description: Ibn Tufayl, Andalusian polymath and court physician to the Almohad ruler, composed his famous philosophical novel Hayy ibn Yaqzan. The work explored natural theology and reason through the story of a child raised by a gazelle on an isolated island.
outcome: The philosophical novel influenced both Islamic and later European Enlightenment thought on natural religion and reason.

title: Death of Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani
description: Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani, founder of the Qadiriyya Sufi order, died in Baghdad after decades of teaching and spiritual guidance. His order became one of the most widespread Sufi tariqas, spreading from North Africa to Southeast Asia.
outcome: The Qadiriyya order became one of Islam's most geographically extensive Sufi networks.

title: Almohad conquest of Seville
description: The Almohads under Abu Yaqub Yusuf completed their conquest of Seville from local Andalusian rulers. This consolidated Almohad control over southern Iberia and marked their expansion into al-Andalus.
outcome: Seville became a major Almohad administrative center and cultural capital in Iberia.

title: Death of Nur al-Din Zangi
description: Nur al-Din Mahmud Zangi, architect of Muslim unity against the Crusaders, died in Damascus. His death created a power vacuum that allowed his subordinate Saladin to take control of Syria and Egypt.
outcome: His death enabled Saladin's rise and the unification of Egypt and Syria under Ayyubid rule.

title: Kilij Arslan II defeats Manuel Komnenos at Myriokephalon
description: The Seljuk Sultan of Rum, Kilij Arslan II, ambushed and defeated the Byzantine Emperor Manuel I Komnenos at the mountain pass of Myriokephalon. The victory secured Seljuk Anatolia against Byzantine reconquest.
outcome: The battle permanently established Seljuk Turkish control over central Anatolia.

title: Construction of the Giralda tower begins in Seville
description: The Almohads began construction of the monumental minaret of the Great Mosque of Seville, known as the Giralda. Designed as both a religious and symbolic structure, it showcased Almohad architectural ambition.
outcome: The Giralda became an iconic example of Almohad architecture and survives today as part of Seville Cathedral.

title: Death of Ibn Mardanish, last independent ruler of Valencia
description: Muhammad ibn Sad ibn Mardanish, known to Christians as Rey Lobo, died after decades of maintaining Valencia's independence from both Almohads and Christians. His death led to Almohad annexation of eastern al-Andalus.
outcome: His death ended the last significant independent Muslim taifa state in eastern Iberia.

title: Battle of Arsuf
description: Richard I of England defeated Saladin's forces at Arsuf during the Third Crusade, breaking Muslim attempts to prevent the Crusader march to Jaffa. Despite the tactical victory, Richard could not retake Jerusalem.
sources: Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad, al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya
outcome: The battle demonstrated Crusader military superiority in open combat but failed to achieve strategic objectives.

title: Battle of Chandawar
description: Muhammad of Ghor and his commander Qutb al-Din Aibak defeated the Gahadavala king Jayachandra at Chandawar near Kannauj. The victory opened the Gangetic plain to Ghurid expansion and Muslim rule in northern India.
outcome: The battle secured Muslim control over the critical Gangetic heartland of North India.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Abu al-Walid ibn Rushd, known in Europe as Averroes, died in Marrakesh after years of disgrace under Almohad persecution. His philosophical commentaries on Aristotle profoundly influenced both Islamic and European scholastic thought.
outcome: His works became foundational to European scholasticism and sparked centuries of debate on reason and revelation.

title: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
description: Combined Christian forces of Castile, Aragon, and Navarre decisively defeated the Almohad Caliphate led by Muhammad al-Nasir. This battle marked the beginning of the end for Muslim power in Iberia.
outcome: The defeat shattered Almohad power and accelerated the Christian Reconquista.

title: Hafsid Dynasty established in Ifriqiya
description: Abu Zakariya Yahya declared independence from the Almohads in Tunis, founding the Hafsid dynasty. The Hafsids would rule Tunisia and eastern Algeria for over three centuries.
outcome: The Hafsids became the dominant power in the Maghreb and claimed the Caliphate after Baghdad fell.

title: Battle of Kirina and rise of Mali Empire
description: Sundiata Keita defeated the Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at Kirina, establishing the Mali Empire. The empire became a major center of Islamic learning and trans-Saharan trade.
sources: Ibn Khaldun, Kitab al-Ibar; oral traditions
outcome: The Mali Empire would become the wealthiest and most powerful Islamic state in West Africa.

title: Fall of Cordoba to Ferdinand III
description: King Ferdinand III of Castile conquered Cordoba after a siege, ending over 500 years of Muslim rule in the former Umayyad capital. The Great Mosque was converted into a cathedral.
outcome: The loss of Cordoba was a devastating symbolic blow to Muslim Spain.

title: Battle of Köse Dağ
description: Mongol forces under Bayju defeated the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum led by Kaykhusraw II. The Seljuk state became a Mongol vassal, ending independent Turkish power in Anatolia.
outcome: The defeat fragmented Anatolia into beyliks and set the stage for Ottoman rise.

title: Fall of Seville to Ferdinand III
description: After a sixteen-month siege, Ferdinand III of Castile captured Seville, the last major Muslim city in western Andalusia. Thousands of Muslims fled to Granada or North Africa.
outcome: Only the Nasrid Kingdom of Granada remained of Muslim Spain.

title: Mamluk Sultanate established in Egypt
description: Mamluk soldiers assassinated the last Ayyubid sultan Turanshah and established their own dynasty under Shajar al-Durr and then Aybak. The Mamluks would rule Egypt and Syria for over 250 years.
outcome: The Mamluks became the defenders of Sunni Islam against Mongols and Crusaders.

title: Re-establishment of Abbasid Caliphate in Cairo
description: Mamluk Sultan Baybars installed al-Mustansir II, an Abbasid survivor, as caliph in Cairo. Though largely ceremonial, this gave the Mamluks religious legitimacy.
outcome: The shadow Abbasid caliphate in Cairo legitimized Mamluk rule until 1517.

title: Death of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi
description: The great Persian polymath Nasir al-Din al-Tusi died in Baghdad after establishing the Maragha Observatory. His astronomical works influenced both Islamic and European science.
sources: George Saliba, Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance
outcome: Al-Tusi's planetary models were used by Copernicus centuries later.

title: Marco Polo arrives in China via Mongol territories
description: Venetian merchant Marco Polo reached Kublai Khan's court after traveling through Muslim Central Asia and Persia. His accounts describe thriving Muslim communities across the Silk Road.
outcome: His writings documented extensive Muslim trade networks across Mongol-controlled Asia.

title: Battle of Abulustayn
description: Mamluk Sultan Baybars defeated the Mongol Ilkhanate forces in Anatolia near Elbistan. This victory secured Mamluk dominance in Syria and halted Mongol expansion westward.
outcome: The battle confirmed the Euphrates as the boundary between Mamluk and Mongol spheres.

title: Establishment of Qalawun Hospital in Cairo
description: Mamluk Sultan Qalawun founded a massive hospital complex (bimaristan) in Cairo with free treatment for all. The complex included a madrasa and mausoleum.
sources: Doris Behrens-Abouseif, Cairo of the Mamluks
outcome: The Qalawun complex became a model for Islamic hospital architecture.

title: Khalji Dynasty established in Delhi
description: Jalal ud-Din Firuz Khalji overthrew the Mamluk dynasty in Delhi, founding the Khalji dynasty. His nephew Alauddin Khalji would later expand the sultanate across much of India.
outcome: The Khalji dynasty expanded Muslim power deep into the Indian subcontinent.

title: Construction of Qutb Minar completed
description: The Qutb Minar, begun by Qutb ud-Din Aibak, was completed by Iltutmish in Delhi. At 73 meters, it became the tallest brick minaret in the world.
sources: Finbarr B. Flood, Objects of Translation
outcome: The Qutb Minar remains an iconic symbol of Indo-Islamic architecture.

title: Death of Jalal al-Din Qaratay
description: Jalal al-Din Qaratay, a prominent Seljuk emir and patron of architecture, died in Konya. He had founded the Karatay Madrasa, one of the finest examples of Seljuk architecture.
sources: Scott Redford, The Archaeology of the Frontier in the Medieval Near East
outcome: His madrasa exemplified the sophisticated tile work of Seljuk Anatolia.

title: Completion of the Alhambra Palace begun
description: Muhammad III of Granada expanded the Nasrid palaces on the Alhambra hill. Construction continued throughout the century, creating one of Islamic architecture's masterpieces.
sources: D. Fairchild Ruggles, Gardens, Landscape, and Vision in the Palaces of Islamic Spain
outcome: The Alhambra became the supreme achievement of Moorish architecture in Spain.

title: Establishment of Samudra Pasai Sultanate
description: The Samudra Pasai Sultanate was established in northern Sumatra as the first Islamic state in Southeast Asia. Sultan Malik al-Saleh founded the dynasty after converting to Islam.
sources: Hikayat Raja-Raja Pasai; Ibn Battuta, Rihla
outcome: Samudra Pasai became a key center for spreading Islam throughout the Indonesian archipelago.

title: Battle of La Forbie
description: An army of Khwarezmian mercenaries allied with Egypt defeated the Crusader-Damascus alliance near Gaza. This battle effectively ended Crusader hopes of holding Jerusalem.
sources: Steven Runciman, A History of the Crusades
outcome: The Crusader military orders lost most of their knights, crippling their power in the Levant.

title: Death of Ibn Sab'in
description: The controversial Sufi philosopher Ibn Sab'in died in Mecca. Known for his radical monistic philosophy and alleged correspondence with Emperor Frederick II, his works were often condemned by orthodox scholars.
sources: Claude Addas, Ibn Arabi ou La quête du Soufre Rouge
outcome: His extreme mystical philosophy represented the radical edge of Islamic esotericism.

title: Death of al-Suhrawardi al-Maqtul
description: The executed Sufi master Shihab al-Din Yahya Suhrawardi's illuminationist philosophy gained posthumous influence in the 13th century. His school of Ishraq (Illuminationism) spread throughout Persia and India.
sources: Henry Corbin, The Man of Light in Iranian Sufism
outcome: Suhrawardi's philosophy created a synthesis of Islamic mysticism and ancient Persian wisdom.

title: Alauddin Khalji captures Devagiri
description: Alauddin Khalji, acting as general for his uncle, conquered the Yadava kingdom of Devagiri in the Deccan. This marked the first major Muslim expansion into southern India.
outcome: The conquest opened the Deccan and southern India to Delhi Sultanate control.

title: Death of Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi
description: The Andalusian geographer and historian Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi died in Tunis. His geographical works corrected and updated Ptolemy's maps and described the Muslim world comprehensively.
sources: Fuat Sezgin, Geschichte des arabischen Schrifttums
outcome: His geographical corrections influenced both Islamic and later European cartography.

title: Death of Qadi al-Fadil's al-Asadi
description: The influential Egyptian jurist and scholar Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Asadi died in Cairo. He was a prominent Shafi'i scholar and teacher who trained many of the period's leading ulama.
outcome: His students perpetuated Shafi'i scholarship in the Mamluk domains.

title: Berke Khan converts the Golden Horde to Islam
description: Berke Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan and ruler of the Golden Horde, officially converted to Islam. His conversion made the Golden Horde the first Mongol khanate to adopt Islam.
outcome: The conversion eventually led to the Islamization of the entire Golden Horde and much of the Eurasian steppe.

title: Death of Ahmad al-Badawi
description: The Sufi saint Ahmad al-Badawi died in Tanta, Egypt, where he had founded one of Egypt's most popular Sufi orders. His tomb became a major pilgrimage site.
sources: Michael Winter, Egyptian Society under Ottoman Rule
outcome: The Badawiyya order became one of the largest Sufi brotherhoods in Egypt.

title: Death of Al-Qadi al-Fadil
description: Abd al-Rahim al-Baysani, known as al-Qadi al-Fadil, Saladin's chief secretary and advisor, died in Cairo. He was one of the most influential administrators in Ayyubid Egypt and a master of Arabic prose.
outcome: His administrative reforms and literary style influenced chancery practices across the Islamic world for generations.

title: Death of Qutb al-Din Aibak
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, founder of the Mamluk dynasty in Delhi and former slave commander, died in a polo accident in Lahore. His death led to a succession crisis in the young sultanate.
outcome: His brief reign established the precedent of Turkish Mamluk rule in northern India.

title: Death of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi
description: Muhammad ibn Umar Fakhr al-Din al-Razi, Persian theologian and philosopher, died in Herat. He was renowned for his Quranic commentary Mafatih al-Ghayb and works reconciling Ash'ari theology with rational philosophy.
outcome: His comprehensive tafsir became one of the most influential Quranic commentaries in Sunni scholarship.

title: Death of Jalal al-Din Khwarazm-Shah
description: Jalal al-Din Mingburnu, last Khwarazmian Shah, was killed in Kurdistan after years of resistance against the Mongols. His defeat at the Battle of the Indus in 1221 had marked the end of the Khwarazmian Empire.
outcome: His death ended organized Muslim resistance to the Mongol conquest of Central Asia and Iran.

title: Establishment of the Almohad Library of Marrakesh
description: The Almohad caliph al-Ma'mun established a major royal library in Marrakesh containing thousands of manuscripts. It became a center for collecting scientific and philosophical works from across the Islamic west.
outcome: The library preserved crucial Andalusian and Maghrebi scientific texts during the period of Christian reconquest.

title: Hafsa bint al-Hajj writes poetry collection
description: Hafsa bint al-Hajj al-Rukuniyya, Andalusian poet from Granada, composed her celebrated poems during this period. She was one of the most accomplished female poets of medieval Islamic Spain.
outcome: Her poetry represents the sophisticated literary culture of late Andalusian society.

title: Battle of Sagrajas commemorated in Granada
description: Muhammad I ibn Nasr established the Nasrid dynasty in Granada after taking the city from Ibn Hud. He would rule until 1273, laying foundations for the last Muslim kingdom in Iberia.
outcome: The Nasrid dynasty would preserve Islamic civilization in Iberia for another 252 years.

title: Death of Ibn al-Farid
description: Umar ibn al-Farid, the great Egyptian Sufi poet, died in Cairo. His mystical Arabic poetry, especially his Ta'iyya al-Kubra, expressed profound spiritual love and unity with the divine.
outcome: His poetry became central to Arabic Sufi literature and influenced mystical thought across the Muslim world.

title: Death of Abd al-Latif al-Baghdadi
description: Abd al-Latif al-Baghdadi, polymath physician and historian, died in Baghdad. He wrote extensively on medicine, philosophy, and Egyptian antiquities, including observations of Egyptian monuments and the famine of 1200-1202.
outcome: His empirical observations and medical writings bridged classical and Islamic medicine.

title: Death of al-Malik al-Salih Ayyub
description: Al-Malik al-Salih Najm al-Din Ayyub, last significant Ayyubid sultan of Egypt, died during the Seventh Crusade. His widow Shajar al-Durr and Mamluk commanders concealed his death until the defeat of Louis IX.
outcome: His death precipitated the Mamluk takeover and end of Ayyubid rule in Egypt.

title: Death of Sa'di Shirazi's master Abu Bakr
description: The Persian poet Sa'di witnessed the Mongol destruction of Baghdad while traveling. This experience profoundly influenced his later works, including the Bustan and Gulistan.
outcome: The trauma of Baghdad's fall shaped Persian ethical and literary responses to Mongol devastation.

title: Baybars captures Caesarea
description: Mamluk Sultan Baybars captured the Crusader fortress of Caesarea, dismantling its fortifications. This was part of his systematic campaign to eliminate Crusader strongholds along the Levantine coast.
outcome: The capture accelerated the collapse of Crusader territorial holdings in the Holy Land.

title: Baybars takes Arsuf and Haifa
description: Baybars captured the coastal cities of Arsuf and Haifa from the Crusaders. These conquests further isolated remaining Crusader territories and secured Mamluk control of the Palestinian coast.
outcome: These victories demonstrated Mamluk military superiority and hastened the end of the Crusader states.

title: Marinid Conquest of Marrakesh
description: Abu Yusuf Yaqub ibn Abd al-Haqq of the Marinid dynasty conquered Marrakesh, ending Almohad rule in Morocco. The Marinids would establish Fez as their capital and dominate the western Maghreb.
outcome: The Marinid victory reshaped North African politics and patronage of Islamic scholarship.

title: Death of Shams al-Din al-Samarqandi
description: Muhammad ibn Ashraf al-Samarqandi, Hanafi jurist and theologian, died in Samarkand. His legal manual al-Mizan and theological works were widely studied in Central Asia and India.
outcome: His systematic approach to Hanafi jurisprudence influenced legal education across the eastern Islamic world.

title: Baybars captures Crac des Chevaliers
description: Sultan Baybars besieged and captured the formidable Crusader castle Crac des Chevaliers after a brief siege. The fortress, held by the Knights Hospitaller, was considered nearly impregnable.
outcome: The fall of this strategic fortress marked a decisive turning point in eliminating Crusader military power in Syria.

title: Death of Qutb al-Din al-Shirazi begins
description: Mahmud ibn Masud Qutb al-Din al-Shirazi, Persian astronomer and physician, completed his major work on optics and astronomy. He provided the first correct explanation of the rainbow's formation.
outcome: His optical theories represented a peak of Islamic scientific achievement in explaining natural phenomena.

title: Death of Shams al-Din Muhammad Juwayni
description: Shams al-Din Muhammad Juwayni, powerful Persian vizier of the Ilkhanate, was executed by order of Arghun Khan. His family had dominated Ilkhanid administration under Mongol rule for decades.
outcome: His fall marked a shift in Ilkhanid politics and the declining influence of Persian bureaucratic families.

title: Second Mongol Invasion of Syria Repelled
description: Mamluk Sultan Qalawun defeated a major Mongol invasion at the Battle of Homs. The Ilkhanid army under Möngke Temür was forced to retreat, securing Mamluk control of Syria.
outcome: The victory confirmed the Mamluks as the dominant power in the Levant and protectors of the Islamic heartland.

title: Death of Abaqa Khan and Succession Crisis
description: Abaqa Khan, Ilkhanid ruler who maintained Buddhist-Christian leanings, died and was succeeded by his Muslim brother Ahmad Tekuder. This triggered conflicts over the religious direction of the Ilkhanate.
outcome: Ahmad's brief reign marked the beginning of the Ilkhanate's gradual conversion to Islam.

title: Completion of Ibn Nafis' Medical Works
description: Ala al-Din Ibn al-Nafis, Egyptian physician, completed his comprehensive medical commentary and pulmonary circulation theory. He was the first to describe correctly the circulation of blood through the lungs.
outcome: His discovery of pulmonary circulation preceded European understanding by three centuries.

title: Qalawun captures Tripoli
description: Sultan Qalawun besieged and conquered the Crusader city of Tripoli after a month-long siege. The city's inhabitants were killed or enslaved, and the city was razed.
outcome: The fall of Tripoli left only a handful of Crusader positions remaining in the Levant.

title: Death of Sa'di Shirazi
description: Muslih al-Din Sa'di, the great Persian poet and moralist, died in Shiraz at an advanced age. His works Bustan and Gulistan became classics of Persian literature, blending wisdom with elegant verse.
outcome: His humanistic ethical teachings influenced Persian and Islamic moral thought for centuries.

title: Ghazan Khan becomes Ilkhanid ruler
description: Mahmud Ghazan converted to Islam and became Ilkhanid ruler, taking the Muslim name Mahmud. His conversion marked the official Islamization of the Mongol Ilkhanate in Persia.
outcome: His reign integrated the Mongol elite into Islamic civilization and initiated major administrative reforms.

title: Balban's Successors and Delhi Politics
description: Following the death of Sultan Muiz ud din Qaiqabad, Jalal ud din Firuz Khalji seized power in Delhi, founding the Khalji dynasty. This marked a shift from Turkish Mamluk to Turko-Afghan rule.
outcome: The Khalji takeover demonstrated the fluidity of power in the Delhi Sultanate and began aggressive expansion into the Deccan.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Abu al-Walid Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Rushd, known in the West as Averroes, died in Marrakesh. The great Andalusian polymath had profoundly influenced both Islamic philosophy and medieval European scholasticism through his commentaries on Aristotle.
sources: Ibn Abi Usaybi'a, Uyun al-Anba fi Tabaqat al-Atibba
outcome: His works continued to shape both Islamic and Christian philosophical traditions for centuries.

title: Death of Maimonides in Egypt
description: Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides), the great Jewish philosopher and physician who served at Saladin's court, died in Fustat, Egypt. His medical writings and philosophical works, written largely in Arabic, influenced Muslim, Jewish, and Christian scholars.
outcome: His synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and religious thought shaped interfaith intellectual discourse.

title: Death of Muhammad of Ghor
description: Sultan Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad of Ghor, who had conquered much of northern India and established Muslim rule in Delhi, was assassinated at Dhamiak. His death led to the succession of his slave-general Qutb al-Din Aibak and the consolidation of the Delhi Sultanate.
outcome: His death marked the transition from Ghurid to independent sultanate rule in India.

title: Death of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi's Major Student al-Khunaji
description: Afḍal al-Din al-Khunaji, prominent student of Fakhr al-Din al-Razi and important logician, died. He authored influential works on logic and theology that bridged Avicennan philosophy and Ash'ari kalam.
outcome: His logical works became standard texts in madrasas across the Islamic world.

title: Battle of Muret Impact on Andalusian Politics
description: The Almohad Caliph Muhammad al-Nasir's ally, Peter II of Aragon, was killed at the Battle of Muret against Simon de Montfort. This weakened Almohad influence in the region and accelerated Christian advances in both southern France and Iberia.
outcome: The battle shifted the balance of power in the western Mediterranean against Muslim states.

title: Mongol Destruction of Merv
description: The Mongols under Tolui Khan captured and systematically destroyed the great city of Merv in Khurasan, killing hundreds of thousands. The city, once among the world's largest and a major center of Islamic learning, never fully recovered.
outcome: The destruction of Merv symbolized the devastating Mongol impact on Central Asian Islamic civilization.

title: Battle of Kalka River
description: Mongol forces under Jebe and Subutai defeated a combined army of Rus' princes and their Kipchak allies at the Kalka River. This Mongol reconnaissance in force demonstrated their reach into Eastern Europe and affected Muslim Kipchak populations.
outcome: The battle opened the way for future Mongol conquests in Eastern Europe and displacement of Turkic Muslim populations.

title: Death of Yaqut al-Hamawi
description: Yaqut al-Hamawi, the former Greek slave who became Islam's greatest medieval geographer, died in Aleppo. His monumental geographical dictionary Mu'jam al-Buldan documented the Islamic world before the Mongol destructions.
outcome: His geographical encyclopedia preserved invaluable knowledge of pre-Mongol Islamic civilization.

title: Death of Ibn al-Athir
description: Ali ibn al-Athir, author of the comprehensive history al-Kamil fi'l-Tarikh, died in Mosul. His historical work provided crucial accounts of the Crusades from an Islamic perspective and chronicled events across the Muslim world.
outcome: His historical writings became a primary source for understanding the Crusader period and Ayyubid era.

title: Death of Ibn al-Hajj al-Ishbili
description: Abu al-Qasim Ahmad ibn al-Hajj al-Ishbili, prominent Andalusian astronomer and mathematician who worked in Seville and Marrakesh, died. He made significant contributions to astronomical observation and instrument design during the late Almohad period.
outcome: His work represented the continuation of Andalusian scientific tradition despite Christian reconquest.

title: Mongols Sack Kiev
description: Batu Khan's Mongol forces captured and destroyed Kiev, capital of Kievan Rus'. The campaign displaced numerous Kipchak Turkic tribes, many of whom were Muslim, forcing migrations toward the Caucasus and Anatolia.
outcome: The destruction led to major population movements affecting Muslim communities in Eastern Europe and the western steppes.

title: Death of Muhyi al-Din ibn al-Zamlakani
description: The prominent Shafi'i jurist and theologian from Damascus died. He was known for his works in Islamic jurisprudence and his teaching at the Umayyad Mosque during a period of Ayyubid cultural flourishing.
outcome: His juristic writings contributed to the systematization of Shafi'i fiqh in the Levant.

title: Death of al-Malik al-Mu'azzam Turanshah
description: The last Ayyubid sultan of Egypt, Turanshah, was assassinated by Mamluk officers after only two months of rule. His murder and the brief regency of Shajar al-Durr marked the transition from Ayyubid to Mamluk rule in Egypt.
outcome: His assassination ended Ayyubid rule in Egypt and inaugurated Mamluk dominance.

title: Ferdinand III Conquers Niebla
description: King Ferdinand III of Castile captured the fortified city of Niebla in southwestern Iberia after a prolonged siege. This conquest further reduced Muslim territory in the Algarve region and displaced many of its Muslim inhabitants.
outcome: The fall accelerated Christian consolidation of southwestern Iberia and Muslim emigration to North Africa.

title: Hulagu Destroys Alamut Castle
description: Hulagu Khan's Mongol forces captured and destroyed Alamut, the mountain fortress headquarters of the Nizari Ismaili state in Persia. The Mongols killed the last Nizari Imam Rukn al-Din Khurshah, effectively ending organized Ismaili power in Persia.
outcome: The destruction ended the Nizari Ismaili state but scattered communities survived throughout the Muslim world.

title: Michael VIII Palaiologos Recaptures Constantinople
description: Byzantine Emperor Michael VIII Palaiologos retook Constantinople from Latin Crusader rule, restoring Byzantine authority. This affected Muslim-Byzantine relations and altered the strategic balance in Anatolia where Seljuk and emerging beylik states operated.
outcome: Byzantine restoration altered diplomatic dynamics between Anatolian Muslim states and Constantinople.

title: Marinids Establish Control Over Fez
description: The Marinid dynasty under Abu Yusuf Ya'qub consolidated control over Fez, making it their capital and beginning its transformation into a major center of Islamic learning. They founded the Madrasa al-Saffarin, initiating a tradition of madrasa architecture.
outcome: Fez became a leading center of Maliki scholarship and Maghrebi Islamic culture under Marinid patronage.

title: Death of Nasir al-Din al-Baydawi
description: Abdullah ibn Umar al-Baydawi, author of the influential Quranic commentary Anwar al-Tanzil, died in Tabriz. His tafsir combined traditional exegesis with rational theology and became one of the most widely studied commentaries in Sunni Islam.
outcome: His commentary became a standard text in Islamic education for centuries across the Sunni world.

title: Death of Jalal al-Din al-Rumi's Son Sultan Walad
description: Sultan Walad, son of Jalal al-Din Rumi who organized the Mevlevi Sufi order after his father's death, established the systematic practices of the whirling dervishes. His organizational work in Konya institutionalized his father's teachings.
outcome: His institutionalization ensured the Mevlevi order's survival and spread throughout the Ottoman lands.

title: Death of Sultan Balban of Delhi
description: Ghiyas al-Din Balban, who had ruled the Delhi Sultanate with an iron hand for twenty years, died in Delhi. His strong centralized rule and military reforms had stabilized the sultanate after years of Mongol threats and internal rebellions.
outcome: His death led to succession disputes and temporary weakening of centralized sultanate authority.

title: Second Battle of Homs
description: Mamluk Sultan Qalawun defeated a combined Mongol-Armenian-Georgian force near Homs in Syria. The victory secured Mamluk control over Syria and demonstrated their military superiority over the Ilkhanate.
outcome: The battle confirmed Mamluk dominance in the Levant and halted Mongol expansion westward.

title: Death of Ahmad al-Tifashi
description: Ahmad ibn Yusuf al-Tifashi, renowned scholar of mineralogy and author of works on precious stones, died in Cairo. His Arabic treatise on gemology combined scientific observation with commercial knowledge from across the Islamic trade network.
outcome: His mineralogical works preserved knowledge of Islamic scientific and commercial expertise in gems and minerals.

title: Mongol Invasions of Burma and Muslim Traders
description: Kublai Khan's forces invaded Burma (Pagan Kingdom), disrupting established trade routes where Muslim merchants had been active for decades. The invasions altered commercial networks in Southeast Asia and affected Muslim trading communities in coastal ports.
outcome: The Mongol campaigns reorganized Southeast Asian trade patterns affecting Muslim merchant networks.

title: Death of Sa'ad al-Din al-Fargani
description: The astronomer and engineer Sa'ad al-Din al-Fargani, who worked on astronomical instruments and water-raising devices in Damascus, died. He represented the continuation of Islamic scientific tradition despite the political upheavals of the Mongol era.
outcome: His work maintained astronomical knowledge in the Mamluk realm during a period of scientific transition.

title: Death of Kublai Khan and Ilkhanate Relations
description: The death of Mongol emperor Kublai Khan in China affected the broader Mongol political system, including relations between the Ilkhanate and Yuan China. His passing altered diplomatic dynamics that involved Muslim administrators and traders across the Mongol realms.
outcome: His death began the gradual fragmentation of Mongol unity affecting Muslim communities across Eurasia.

title: Death of Salah al-Din's brother al-Adil I proclaimed Sultan
description: Al-Adil I (al-Malik al-Adil Sayf al-Din) consolidates power over the Ayyubid realm after years of maneuvering following Salah al-Din's death. He ruled from Damascus and Cairo, reuniting much of the Ayyubid territories under his direct control.
outcome: Al-Adil's reign stabilized the Ayyubid state and maintained relative peace with the Crusaders until his death in 1218.

title: Death of Al-Malik al-Adil's rival al-Afdal
description: Al-Afdal ibn Salah al-Din, the eldest son of Saladin who had ruled Damascus, dies after being politically sidelined by his uncle al-Adil. His death ended the succession disputes that had plagued the Ayyubid dynasty since Saladin's death in 1193.
sources: Abu al-Fida, al-Mukhtasar fi Akhbar al-Bashar
outcome: Consolidated Ayyubid power under al-Adil's branch of the family.

title: Death of Muhammad al-Nasir after Las Navas de Tolosa
description: Almohad Caliph Muhammad al-Nasir dies in Marrakesh following his catastrophic defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa the same year. His death accelerated the fragmentation of the Almohad caliphate across North Africa and al-Andalus.
outcome: The Almohad empire entered terminal decline, leading to the rise of successor states like the Hafsids, Marinids, and Nasrids.

title: Death of Ibn al-Salah al-Shahrazuri
description: Abu Amr Uthman ibn al-Salah al-Shahrazuri, the leading hadith scholar and author of Muqaddimah fi Ulum al-Hadith, dies in Damascus. His work on hadith methodology became the foundational text for all subsequent Islamic scholarship on the science of hadith transmission.
outcome: His Muqaddimah standardized hadith criticism and became required study in Islamic institutions for centuries.

title: Mongols Destroy Balkh
description: Mongol forces under Genghis Khan's command destroy the ancient city of Balkh, known as 'Mother of Cities,' during their conquest of Khwarazm. The city's population was massacred and its libraries and monuments razed.
outcome: Balkh never recovered its former prominence as a major center of Islamic learning and trade.

title: Death of Ibn al-Qifti
description: Jamal al-Din al-Qifti, the Egyptian vizier and author of Tarikh al-Hukama (History of Learned Men), dies in Aleppo. His biographical dictionary preserved invaluable information about Greek and Islamic philosophers and scientists.
outcome: His work became a primary source for the history of science and philosophy in the Islamic world.

title: Construction of the Great Mosque of Djenne
description: Sultan Koi Konboro of the Mali Empire commissions the construction of the Great Mosque of Djenne in present-day Mali. This became one of the most significant examples of Sudano-Sahelian architecture and a major center of Islamic learning in West Africa.
outcome: Djenne became a major center of Islamic scholarship alongside Timbuktu in the trans-Saharan trade network.

title: Death of Ibn al-Baytar
description: Abu Muhammad Abdallah Ibn Ahmad Ibn al-Baytar, the Andalusian botanist and pharmacologist, dies in Damascus. His comprehensive botanical encyclopedia al-Jami li-Mufradat al-Adwiya wa'l-Aghdhiya described over 1,400 plants and their medicinal uses.
outcome: His work remained the most authoritative pharmacological reference in the Islamic world and Europe until the early modern period.

title: Consecration of Kutubiyya Mosque Minaret
description: The completion and consecration of the Kutubiyya Mosque minaret in Marrakesh under the late Almohad period marks the perfection of Moroccan-Andalusian architectural style. Standing 77 meters tall, it became the prototype for the Giralda in Seville and Hassan Tower in Rabat.
outcome: The Kutubiyya became the defining symbol of Marrakesh and influenced mosque architecture across the Maghreb.

title: Marinids Capture Marrakesh and End Almohad Rule
description: Abu Yusuf Yaqub ibn Abd al-Haqq of the Marinid dynasty captures Marrakesh from the last Almohad caliph al-Wathiq, ending Almohad rule in Morocco. The Marinids established their capital in Fez and became the dominant power in the western Maghreb.

figures.name: Abu Yusuf Yaqub ibn Abd al-Haqq

outcome: The Marinids revitalized Moroccan power and sponsored a cultural renaissance in Fez.

title: Death of Shaykh Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili
description: Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili, founder of the Shadhiliyya Sufi order, dies in Humaythara, Egypt, while on pilgrimage. His tariqa emphasized spiritual remembrance without abandoning worldly responsibilities and became one of the most widespread Sufi orders in North Africa.
sources: Ibn Ata Allah al-Iskandari, Lata'if al-Minan
outcome: The Shadhiliyya order spread throughout North Africa, Egypt, and later the Ottoman world, emphasizing practical spirituality.

title: Death of Ibn Khallikan
description: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Khallikan, author of Wafayat al-A'yan (Obituaries of Eminent Men), dies in Damascus. His biographical dictionary became one of the most important sources for Islamic cultural history, covering over 800 notable figures.
outcome: His biographical work preserved detailed information about medieval Islamic civilization and remains a primary historical source.

title: Death of Ibn Daqiq al-Eid
description: Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Daqiq al-Eid, the great Egyptian muhaddith and Shafi'i jurist, dies in Cairo. He combined expertise in hadith, fiqh, and Arabic language, and his commentary on hadith collections influenced generations of scholars.
outcome: His methodology in reconciling hadith and fiqh became foundational in Shafi'i jurisprudence.

title: Kaykaus III Flees to Constantinople
description: Kaykaus III, the last Seljuk Sultan of Rum, flees Anatolia to seek refuge in Byzantine Constantinople after Mongol Ilkhanid pressure. The Byzantine emperor Andronikos II granted him territories in Thrace, marking the effective end of Seljuk sovereignty in Anatolia.
outcome: This opened the way for the rise of Anatolian beyliks, including the nascent Ottoman state under Osman I.

title: Establishment of the Ottoman beylik as independent
description: Osman I declared independence from the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum as it disintegrated under Mongol pressure. This marked the formal beginning of the Ottoman state as a sovereign entity in northwestern Anatolia.
outcome: The Ottoman state emerged as an independent power that would eventually become one of history's greatest empires.

title: Battle of Marj al-Saffar
description: Mamluk Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad defeated the Mongol Ilkhanate forces near Damascus. This victory secured Mamluk control over Syria and ended Mongol attempts to conquer the region.
outcome: The battle marked the final Mongol attempt to invade Syria and confirmed Mamluk dominance in the Levant.

title: Completion of the Alhambra's Generalife Gardens
description: Sultan Ismail I of Granada commissioned the completion of the Generalife palace and gardens. The complex became a masterpiece of Nasrid architecture and hydraulic engineering.
sources: Ibn al-Khatib, al-Ihata fi Akhbar Gharnata
outcome: The Generalife remains one of the finest examples of Islamic garden architecture in Europe.

title: Mansa Musa becomes Emperor of Mali
description: Mansa Musa ascended to the throne of the Mali Empire following the disappearance of his predecessor Abu Bakr II. He would transform Mali into one of the wealthiest and most influential Islamic states in Africa.
sources: Ibn Fadl Allah al-Umari, Masalik al-Absar
outcome: Mansa Musa's reign marked the golden age of the Mali Empire and its prominence in trans-Saharan trade.

title: Death of Amir Khusrau
description: The great Indo-Persian poet and musician Amir Khusrau died in Delhi. He was a disciple of the Sufi saint Nizamuddin Auliya and is credited with developing Qawwali music and enriching Hindustani classical music.
outcome: Amir Khusrau's literary and musical innovations profoundly shaped Indo-Islamic culture for centuries.

title: Mansa Musa's Hajj to Mecca
description: Mansa Musa embarked on his legendary pilgrimage to Mecca with a caravan of 60,000 people and immense quantities of gold. His lavish spending in Cairo and Mecca made him famous across the Islamic world and Europe.
outcome: The pilgrimage established Mali's reputation for wealth and brought Mali into closer contact with the central Islamic lands.

title: Death of Osman I
description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman dynasty, died and was succeeded by his son Orhan. Under Osman, the small beylik had grown from a frontier principality into a significant Anatolian power.
outcome: Osman's death transferred power to Orhan who would continue the Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans.

title: Construction of the Sultan Hassan Mosque begins in Cairo
description: Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad initiated extensive building projects in Cairo including mosques and madrasas. His architectural patronage transformed Cairo into one of the Islamic world's most magnificent cities.
outcome: Cairo's architectural grandeur during al-Nasir Muhammad's reign established it as a major center of Islamic art and learning.

title: Foundation of the Vijayanagara Empire
description: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I established the Vijayanagara Empire in South India, creating a Hindu kingdom that would resist Delhi Sultanate expansion. The empire would engage in complex diplomatic and military relations with Muslim states for over two centuries.
sources: Persian chronicles of the Bahmani Sultanate
outcome: The empire's rise changed the balance of power in South India and created a major rival to Muslim sultanates.

title: Death of Nizamuddin Auliya
description: The great Chishti Sufi saint Nizamuddin Auliya died in Delhi after a lifetime of teaching and spiritual guidance. His teachings emphasized love, tolerance, and service to humanity regardless of religious background.
outcome: His shrine became one of the most important pilgrimage sites in South Asia, transcending religious boundaries.

title: Foundation of the Bahmani Sultanate
description: Alauddin Hasan Bahman Shah, a former governor, declared independence from the Delhi Sultanate and established the Bahmani Sultanate in the Deccan. This marked the beginning of Muslim political independence in South India.
outcome: The Bahmani Sultanate became a major center of Persian-Islamic culture in the Deccan for over a century.

title: Ottomans cross into Europe at Gallipoli
description: Ottoman forces under Orhan's son Süleyman Pasha crossed the Dardanelles and captured Gallipoli following an earthquake that damaged the fortress. This established the first permanent Ottoman foothold in Europe.
outcome: The capture of Gallipoli opened the way for Ottoman expansion into the Balkans.

title: Ottomans capture Adrianople
description: Sultan Murad I captured Adrianople (Edirne) from the Byzantines, making it the new Ottoman capital. The conquest gave the Ottomans control of the strategic crossroads between Anatolia and the Balkans.
outcome: Edirne served as the Ottoman capital for nearly a century and symbolized Ottoman commitment to European expansion.

title: Construction of the Green Mosque in Bursa begins
description: Ottoman architectural patronage flourished in Bursa with the construction of major mosques and complexes. The city became an important center of early Ottoman Islamic art and architecture.
outcome: Bursa's architectural heritage established the foundations of classical Ottoman style.

title: Conversion of Sultan of Malacca's predecessor
description: Muslim traders from India and Arabia increasingly established communities in Southeast Asian ports. The process of Islamization in the Malay archipelago accelerated through trade networks and Sufi missionaries.
outcome: The gradual conversion of Southeast Asian rulers laid the groundwork for Islam's dominance in the region.

title: Ibn Khaldun serves as Maliki Chief Judge of Cairo
description: Ibn Khaldun was appointed as the Maliki chief judge (qadi) of Cairo by the Mamluk sultan. He served with distinction but faced political challenges due to his independent judgments.
outcome: His judicial career demonstrated the practical application of his theories about governance and social organization.

title: Death of the historian al-Safadi
description: The renowned Mamluk historian and biographer Salah al-Din al-Safadi died in Damascus. He compiled the massive biographical dictionary al-Wafi bi'l-Wafayat, documenting thousands of Islamic scholars and notable figures.
outcome: His biographical works remain essential sources for Islamic intellectual history of the 13th and 14th centuries.

title: Barquq establishes the Burji Mamluk dynasty
description: Circassian Mamluk commander Barquq seized power in Cairo, ending the Bahri dynasty and establishing the Burji Mamluk sultanate. This transition marked a shift in the ethnic composition of the Mamluk military elite.
outcome: The Burji dynasty would rule Egypt and Syria until the Ottoman conquest in 1517.

title: Battle of Savra
description: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated a Serbian-Bosnian coalition at Savra. The victory consolidated Ottoman control over much of the southern Balkans and Macedonia.
outcome: The battle further weakened Serbian resistance and paved the way for deeper Ottoman penetration into Europe.

title: Establishment of Janissary corps reforms
description: The Ottoman Janissary corps, originally formed from devshirme (levy of Christian boys), was formally organized into a professional standing army. This elite infantry became central to Ottoman military success.
outcome: The Janissaries became one of the most effective military forces in the world for centuries.

title: Fall of Granada's Mohammed VI
description: Sultan Mohammed VI of Granada was overthrown after a reign marked by internal strife and Christian pressure. The constant palace coups weakened the Nasrid dynasty as Castile expanded.
outcome: The political instability in Granada hastened the eventual Christian conquest of the last Muslim state in Iberia.

title: Timur conquers Baghdad
description: Timur captured Baghdad from the Jalayirid dynasty, massacring much of the population. The conquest marked Timur's domination of Mesopotamia and Persia.
outcome: The sack further devastated Baghdad, which had never fully recovered from the Mongol destruction of 1258.

title: Construction of Bibi-Khanym Mosque in Samarkand begins
description: Timur ordered the construction of the grand Bibi-Khanym Mosque in Samarkand to commemorate his Indian campaign. The mosque was intended to be one of the largest and most magnificent in the Islamic world.
outcome: The mosque exemplified Timurid architectural ambition and artistic synthesis of Persian and Central Asian styles.

title: Timur invades Syria
description: Timur launched a campaign into Mamluk Syria, capturing Aleppo and devastating several cities. His forces massacred thousands and carried off skilled artisans to Samarkand.
outcome: The invasion weakened the Mamluk Sultanate and demonstrated Timur's military reach across the Islamic world.

title: Establishment of the Malacca Sultanate
description: Parameswara founded the Malacca Sultanate after fleeing from Palembang. Under his rule and that of his successors, Malacca converted to Islam and became a major center of Islamic learning and trade in Southeast Asia.
outcome: The Malacca Sultanate became the most important center for the spread of Islam in maritime Southeast Asia.

title: Death of Sultan al-Mansur Qalawun's successor al-Nasir Muhammad's first reign ends
description: Al-Nasir Muhammad ibn Qalawun was briefly deposed for the first time, beginning a complex period of Mamluk political instability. This marked the start of his tumultuous relationship with Mamluk amirs who would depose and restore him multiple times.
outcome: Al-Nasir Muhammad would return to power twice more, eventually ruling for 31 years total and overseeing a golden age of Mamluk architecture.

title: Battle of Marj al-Suffar
description: Mamluk forces under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad defeated a major Mongol Ilkhanid invasion near Damascus. This decisive victory ended Mongol attempts to conquer Syria and secured Mamluk dominance in the Levant.
outcome: The battle permanently ended Mongol threats to Mamluk Syria and established the Mamluks as the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.

title: Completion of the Mihrimah Sultan Mosque in Üsküdar
description: Construction of early Ottoman religious architecture continued in Bursa's surrounding regions under Osman I's reign. These early mosques established architectural patterns that would influence later Ottoman imperial style.
outcome: Early Ottoman architecture began developing distinctive features blending Seljuk and Byzantine influences.

title: Death of the poet Awhad al-Din Anvari's successor Saadi al-Din Mahmud Shabistari
description: Mahmud Shabistari, Persian Sufi poet, composed the Gulshan-i Raz (The Rose Garden of Mystery), one of the most celebrated mystical poems in Persian literature. The work presented Sufi metaphysics in poetic form.
outcome: The Gulshan-i Raz became one of the most commented-upon Sufi texts, influencing Persian mystical literature for centuries.

title: Death of Sultan Mansa Qu
description: Mansa Qu, ruler of the Mali Empire, died after a brief reign. His death preceded the accession of Mansa Musa, who would become Mali's most famous emperor.
outcome: The succession paved the way for Mansa Musa's transformative reign over Mali.

title: Marriage alliance between the Ilkhanate and the Golden Horde
description: Sultan Öljaitü of the Ilkhanate arranged a marriage alliance with Özbeg Khan of the Golden Horde, temporarily easing tensions between the two Mongol successor states. This reflected the Ilkhanate's conversion to Islam influencing inter-Mongol relations.
outcome: The alliance temporarily reduced conflicts between Muslim Mongol states, though rivalries would resume after Öljaitü's death.

title: Death of Sultan Öljaitü
description: Öljaitü, the Ilkhanid ruler who converted from Buddhism to Shi'a Islam, died in Sultaniyya. His theological shifts and patronage of Persian arts marked a significant phase in Ilkhanid history.
outcome: His death led to succession by his son Abu Sa'id, who would be the last strong Ilkhanid ruler before the dynasty's fragmentation.

title: Nasrid Sultan Nasr abdicates in Granada
description: Sultan Nasr of Granada was forced to abdicate in favor of his nephew Ismail I after internal conflicts weakened his position. Ismail I would initiate a brief renaissance of Nasrid power.
sources: Ibn al-Khatib, al-Ihata fi akhbar Gharnata
outcome: Ismail I's accession brought military successes against Castile and a period of cultural flourishing in Granada.

title: Death of Kaykaus II's descendant Mas'ud III as last Seljuk Sultan of Rum
description: The Seljuk Sultanate of Rum effectively ended as a political entity as the last nominal sultans lost all real power to Mongol Ilkhanid overlords and emerging Turkish beyliks. This power vacuum allowed the rise of the Ottomans and other Anatolian principalities.
outcome: The end of Seljuk authority fragmented Anatolia into competing beyliks, creating conditions for Ottoman expansion.

title: Treaty between Granada and Castile
description: Sultan Ismail I of Granada negotiated a peace treaty with Castile after successful military campaigns. The treaty temporarily secured Granada's borders and provided diplomatic breathing room.
outcome: The treaty provided Granada with a decade of relative stability before renewed Castilian pressure.

title: Foundation of the Majapahit Empire
description: The Hindu-Buddhist Majapahit Empire was founded in Java by Raden Wijaya, creating a major regional power that would interact extensively with Muslim traders. This empire would later facilitate the spread of Islam in maritime Southeast Asia through trade networks.
outcome: Majapahit became the dominant maritime power in Southeast Asia, with Muslim merchants playing crucial roles in its trade networks.

title: Death of Ibn Taymiyyah
description: The influential Hanbali scholar Taqi al-Din Ibn Taymiyyah died in a Damascus prison where he had been held for his controversial theological positions. His works on Islamic law and theology would profoundly influence later reform movements.
outcome: Ibn Taymiyyah's literalist approach to Islamic texts became foundational for later Salafi movements and continues to influence Islamic thought.

title: Death of Abu Sa'id and collapse of the Ilkhanate
description: Abu Sa'id Bahadur Khan, the last major Ilkhanid ruler, died without a clear heir, leading to the rapid fragmentation of the Ilkhanate into rival regional dynasties. His death ended Mongol unity in Persia.
outcome: The Ilkhanate's collapse fragmented Persia into competing dynasties including the Jalayirids, Muzaffarids, and others, setting the stage for Timur's later conquests.

title: Al-Nasir Muhammad's death ends Mamluk golden age
description: Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad died after his third and longest reign of 31 years, during which he oversaw massive building projects and economic prosperity. His death initiated a period of rapid sultan turnover and instability.
outcome: After his death, twelve sultans ruled in the next 45 years, marking a period of Mamluk political instability despite continued cultural flourishing.

title: Battle of Rio Salado
description: A combined Castilian-Portuguese force defeated a Marinid-Nasrid coalition at Rio Salado near Tarifa. This major Christian victory ended the last significant North African military intervention in Iberia.
outcome: The battle decisively ended Marinid involvement in Iberian affairs and accelerated the Christian Reconquista.

title: Castilian conquest of Algeciras
description: Alfonso XI of Castile conquered the strategic port city of Algeciras after a lengthy siege, severing a key connection between Granada and North Africa. This victory significantly weakened the Nasrid Emirate's ability to receive reinforcements.
outcome: The loss of Algeciras isolated Granada from North African support and marked a turning point in the Reconquista.

title: Madurai Sultanate reaches its zenith under Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad Damghani
description: The Madurai Sultanate in South India reached its greatest extent under Sultan Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad Damghani. This Muslim dynasty had been established by Delhi Sultanate generals who declared independence.
outcome: The sultanate controlled much of Tamil Nadu before being absorbed by the Vijayanagara Empire later in the century.

title: Establishment of the Marinid necropolis at Chellah
description: Sultan Abu al-Hasan Ali of the Marinid dynasty established the royal necropolis at Chellah near Rabat, building over Roman ruins. The site featured elaborate architectural decoration typical of Marinid style.
outcome: Chellah became an important pilgrimage site and exemplar of Marinid architectural achievement in Morocco.

title: Completion of the Alhambra's Palace of the Lions
description: Sultan Muhammad V of Granada completed the Palace of the Lions (Palacio de los Leones) in the Alhambra, featuring its famous fountain supported by twelve marble lions. This masterpiece represented the pinnacle of Nasrid architectural achievement.
outcome: The Palace of the Lions became the most celebrated example of Islamic architecture in Spain and influenced architectural styles across the Mediterranean.

title: Shams al-Din Ilyas Shah establishes Bengal Sultanate independence
description: Shams al-Din Ilyas Shah consolidated control over Bengal and Bihar, establishing a truly independent Bengal Sultanate free from Delhi's control. He took the title 'Shah of Bengal' and made Pandua his capital.
outcome: The Ilyas Shahi dynasty ruled Bengal for over a century, presiding over a period of prosperity and cultural development.

title: Completion of the Alcazar of Seville under Pedro I
description: Pedro I of Castile completed the Mudéjar-style palace in Seville using Muslim craftsmen from Granada. The palace exemplified the Mudéjar style blending Islamic and Christian architectural elements.
outcome: The Alcazar demonstrated the continued influence of Islamic architectural traditions in Christian Spain and preserved Nasrid artistic techniques.

title: Death of scholar and traveler Ibn Battuta
description: The Moroccan scholar Ibn Battuta died in Fez after dictating his famous Rihla (travels) to Ibn Juzayy. His journeys had covered over 75,000 miles across the Islamic world and beyond.
outcome: His Rihla became one of the most important sources for 14th-century Islamic world history and geography.

title: Muzaffarid dynasty defeated by Timur
description: Timur conquered Isfahan and eliminated the Muzaffarid dynasty that had ruled central Persia since the Ilkhanate's collapse. Shah Shuja and Shah Mahmud were defeated, consolidating Timurid control over Persia.
outcome: The conquest of the Muzaffarids gave Timur control over Persia's heartland and its cultural centers.

title: Death of the poet Hafez in Shiraz
description: Khwaja Shams al-Din Muhammad Hafez-e Shirazi, one of the greatest Persian lyric poets, died in Shiraz. His Divan of poetry, celebrating love, wine, and mysticism, became the most beloved work in Persian literature.
outcome: Hafez's poetry profoundly influenced Persian culture and continues to be widely read and memorized throughout the Persian-speaking world.

title: Timur's massacre at Sivas
description: Timur captured the Anatolian city of Sivas and massacred its population, burying thousands alive. This atrocity was part of his campaign against the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I and various Anatolian beyliks.
outcome: The massacre demonstrated Timur's brutality and foreshadowed his devastating invasion of Ottoman territories.

title: Battle of Bapheus
description: Ottoman forces under Osman I defeated a Byzantine army near Nicomedia in their first major victory against the Byzantines. This battle established Ottoman control over the Bithynian countryside and opened the way for further expansion into Byzantine territory.
outcome: Established Ottoman military dominance in northwestern Anatolia and marked the beginning of Byzantine territorial losses in Asia Minor.

title: Death of Ghazan Khan
description: Ilkhanid ruler Ghazan Khan, who converted to Islam and reformed the administration of Persia, died after a reign that strengthened Islamic institutions. His conversion had marked a turning point for the Mongol Ilkhanate, transforming it into an Islamic state.
outcome: His reign consolidated Islam as the official religion of the Ilkhanate and modernized Persian administration.

title: Construction of the Soltaniyeh Dome
description: Sultan Öljaitü completed the magnificent dome of Soltaniyeh in Persia, one of the largest brick domes ever constructed. The mausoleum represents the pinnacle of Ilkhanid architecture with its innovative double-shell dome structure.
outcome: The dome influenced later Persian and Ottoman architecture and remains a UNESCO World Heritage site.

title: Mansa Abubakari II's Atlantic Expedition
description: Mansa Abubakari II of Mali abdicated to personally lead an expedition of 2,000 ships to explore the Atlantic Ocean, never to return. His departure allowed Mansa Musa to ascend to the throne of Mali.
outcome: This mysterious expedition represents African maritime ambition and led to Mansa Musa's legendary reign.

title: Death of Rashid al-Din
description: The great Persian historian and vizier Rashid al-Din was executed on false charges in Tabriz after decades of service to the Ilkhanate. His monumental work Jami' al-tawarikh remains one of the most important sources for medieval Islamic and world history.
outcome: Despite his tragic death, his historical works preserved crucial knowledge of Mongol, Islamic, and world history.

title: Treaty of Paris between France and Flanders
description: The Marinid Sultan Abu Sa'id Uthman II sent diplomatic envoys to European courts as part of ongoing Nasrid-Marinid efforts to counter Christian expansion in Iberia. These diplomatic missions represented Muslim engagement with broader European politics.
outcome: Marinid diplomacy sought to support Granada but ultimately could not prevent Christian advances in Iberia.

title: Muhammad bin Tughluq becomes Sultan of Delhi
description: Muhammad bin Tughluq ascended to the throne of the Delhi Sultanate after the death of his father Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq. His reign would be marked by ambitious administrative experiments including the attempted move of the capital to Daulatabad.
outcome: His controversial policies and administrative experiments destabilized the sultanate despite his intellectual brilliance.

title: Death of Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad's rival Abu Bakr
description: After al-Nasir Muhammad's death in 1341, a period of instability began, but in 1331 he was still consolidating power. Al-Nasir Muhammad suppressed various rivals and governed Egypt during its cultural and economic peak.
outcome: Al-Nasir Muhammad's long reign represented the zenith of Mamluk prosperity and architectural patronage.

title: Death of Alauddin Khalji's successor Ghiyasuddin Tughluq establishes new dynasty
description: The Tughluq dynasty firmly established itself in Delhi, with architectural projects including the fortress city of Tughlaqabad. Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq built massive fortifications that still stand today.
outcome: The Tughluqs ruled Delhi for nearly a century despite internal challenges and ambitious policies.

title: Death of the Sufi master Baha-ud-Din Naqshband's predecessor Khwaja Ali Ramitani
description: Khwaja Ali Ramitani, a prominent master in the Khwajagan Sufi lineage in Central Asia, died after training numerous disciples. His teachings would influence the later development of the Naqshbandi order.
outcome: His spiritual lineage would produce the Naqshbandi order, one of the major Sufi brotherhoods.

title: Castilian naval victory at Algeciras
description: After the conquest of the city, Castilian forces consolidated control over the Strait of Gibraltar region. This represented a major blow to Marinid-Nasrid cooperation in maintaining Muslim control of southern Iberia.
outcome: Control of Algeciras gave Christian kingdoms dominance over the strait and isolated Granada.

title: Establishment of the Madurai Sultanate's independence
description: The Madurai Sultanate in southern India asserted its independence from the weakening Delhi Sultanate. Jalal ud-Din Ahsan Shah established a distinct Muslim state in Tamil country.
outcome: The sultanate represented Muslim political presence in deep South India until its fall in 1378.

title: Death of the scholar and jurist Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya
description: The prominent Hanbali scholar and student of Ibn Taymiyyah died in Damascus after producing influential works on Islamic jurisprudence and theology. His writings on the principles of Islamic law and spiritual purification remain widely studied.
outcome: His theological and legal writings influenced later Islamic reform movements and scholarship.

title: Firuz Shah Tughluq becomes Sultan of Delhi
description: Firuz Shah Tughluq ascended to the throne and initiated major irrigation projects, administrative reforms, and architectural works across northern India. His relatively peaceful reign saw the construction of numerous canals, mosques, and madrasas.
outcome: His reign brought stability to the Delhi Sultanate and saw major investments in infrastructure and Islamic institutions.

title: Murad I becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Murad I succeeded his father Orhan as Ottoman sultan and dramatically expanded Ottoman territories in both Anatolia and the Balkans. His reign saw the organization of the Ottoman state and military on more permanent foundations.
outcome: Murad I transformed the Ottomans from a beylik into a major empire spanning two continents.

title: Crusader sack of Alexandria
description: A crusading expedition led by Peter I of Cyprus attacked and briefly captured Alexandria, causing massive destruction. Mamluk forces under Sultan al-Ashraf Sha'ban quickly recaptured the city but significant damage had been done to this major Mediterranean port.
outcome: The attack temporarily disrupted Mediterranean trade but failed to establish permanent Christian control.

title: Timur consolidates power in Transoxiana
description: Timur established himself as the undisputed ruler of Transoxiana after defeating his rivals and former allies. From Samarkand, he began building the base for his future conquests across Asia.
outcome: This consolidation launched Timur's career as one of history's most successful conquerors.

title: Death of Taj al-Din al-Subki
description: The prominent Shafi'i scholar and Chief Judge of Damascus, Taj al-Din al-Subki, died after a distinguished career defending Ash'ari theology. His major work Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra remains an essential source for Shafi'i biographical history.
outcome: His theological works strengthened Ash'ari doctrine and his biographical work preserved centuries of Shafi'i scholarship.

title: Fall of the Madurai Sultanate
description: The Madurai Sultanate in southern India fell to the expanding Vijayanagara Empire, ending Muslim rule in the Tamil country. The conquest represented Vijayanagara's consolidation of power in South India.
outcome: The fall of Madurai eliminated the last independent Muslim sultanate in far southern India.

title: Tokhtamysh becomes Khan of the Golden Horde
description: With Timur's support, Tokhtamysh reunified the Golden Horde after decades of fragmentation. His rule would later bring him into conflict with his former patron Timur.
outcome: The reunification temporarily restored Golden Horde power but set the stage for future conflict with Timur.

title: Battle of Savra (Second Battle)
description: Ottoman forces under Murad I defeated Serbian and Bosnian armies, consolidating Ottoman control over the central Balkans. This victory paved the way for the decisive Battle of Kosovo four years later.
outcome: The battle accelerated Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and Serbian submission.

title: Bayezid I becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Following his father Murad I's death at Kosovo, Bayezid I ascended to the throne and earned the title 'Yildirim' (Thunderbolt) for his rapid military campaigns. He accelerated Ottoman expansion in both Anatolia and the Balkans.
outcome: His aggressive expansion brought the Ottomans into direct conflict with Timur and European powers.

title: Timur's invasion of the Golden Horde
description: Timur invaded the territories of his former protégé Tokhtamysh, devastating the Golden Horde's lands in a punitive campaign. The conflict arose from Tokhtamysh's raids into Timur's territories and his independent ambitions.
outcome: The invasion permanently weakened the Golden Horde and established Timurid dominance in Central Asia.

title: Timur conquers Baghdad (second capture)
description: Timur captured Baghdad from the Jalayirid dynasty, consolidating his control over Iraq and Mesopotamia. The conquest included significant destruction but also integrated Iraq into Timur's growing empire.
outcome: The conquest eliminated the Jalayirid dynasty and brought Mesopotamia under Timurid control.

title: Yusuf III becomes Sultan of Granada
description: Yusuf III ascended to the Nasrid throne of Granada during a period of intense internal conflict and Christian pressure. His reign would see continued struggles to maintain Granada's independence against Castilian expansion.
outcome: His relatively stable reign provided Granada a respite before its eventual fall in 1492.

title: Death of the Andalusian scholar Ibn al-Khatib's predecessor Abu Ishaq al-Shatibi
description: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim al-Shatibi al-Gharanati, a renowned Maliki jurist and legal theorist from Granada, passed away. His works on Islamic jurisprudence and legal theory influenced subsequent Andalusian scholarship, particularly in the field of maqasid al-shari'ah (objectives of Islamic law).
outcome: His legal methodology influenced later Maliki scholarship in North Africa and Andalusia.

title: Construction of the Qutb Minar complex completion under Alauddin Khalji
description: The Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque expansion and final additions to the Qutb Minar complex in Delhi were completed under Sultan Alauddin Khalji. The Alai Darwaza gateway, a masterpiece of Indo-Islamic architecture, was finished as part of this project.
outcome: The complex became a defining monument of the Delhi Sultanate's architectural legacy.

title: Death of Sultan Musa I of Mali's predecessor Abu Bakr II
description: Sultan Abu Bakr II of Mali abdicated the throne to undertake an Atlantic exploration expedition with a fleet of vessels, never to return. This led to Mansa Musa I ascending to power and establishing the golden age of the Mali Empire.
outcome: His disappearance paved the way for Mansa Musa's legendary reign.

title: Completion of the Congregational Mosque of Herat
description: The Friday Mosque of Herat in Afghanistan underwent major reconstruction and completion under the Kartid dynasty. The mosque became one of the most important religious centers in Khorasan during this period.
outcome: The mosque served as a major center of Islamic learning in eastern Persia for centuries.

title: Completion of the Tomb of Oljaytu at Soltaniyeh
description: The magnificent mausoleum of Ilkhanid Sultan Oljaytu in Soltaniyeh was completed, featuring the largest brick dome in the world at the time. The structure represents the pinnacle of Ilkhanid architectural achievement with its innovative double-shell dome construction.
outcome: The dome influenced Persian architectural design for centuries and remains a UNESCO World Heritage site.

title: Establishment of Tlemcen as capital under Abul Hassan Ali
description: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Othman became Sultan of the Marinid dynasty and made Tlemcen a major cultural center. He embarked on ambitious construction projects and expanded Marinid influence across the Maghreb.
outcome: Tlemcen became a major center of trade and Islamic scholarship in North Africa.

title: Founding of the Suhrawardi Khanqah in Multan
description: A major Suhrawardi Sufi lodge was established in Multan by Shah Rukn-e-Alam's successors, becoming one of the most important centers of Sufi learning in South Asia. The khanqah attracted disciples from across the Indian subcontinent and Central Asia.
outcome: Multan became a major pilgrimage center and seat of Suhrawardi Sufi influence in South Asia.

title: Conquest of the Canary Islands attempted by Marinid fleet
description: The Marinid Sultan Abu al-Hasan dispatched a naval expedition to the Canary Islands as part of efforts to extend Muslim influence in the Atlantic. The expedition established temporary presence but faced resistance from indigenous populations.
outcome: The expedition demonstrated Marinid naval ambitions but did not result in lasting control.

title: Battle of Qarabagh between rival Ilkhanid successors
description: Following the collapse of the Ilkhanate, rival factions fought for control in the Battle of Qarabagh in Azerbaijan. The Chobanid and Jalayirid dynasties clashed as central authority disintegrated across Persia.
outcome: The battle exemplified the fragmentation of Mongol power in Persia before Timur's consolidation.

title: Death of the scholar Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya
description: Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya, the prominent Hanbali scholar and student of Ibn Taymiyyah, died in Damascus. His extensive works on Islamic jurisprudence, theology, and spirituality profoundly influenced later Islamic thought.
outcome: His works became foundational texts in Hanbali jurisprudence and Islamic spirituality.

title: Founding of the Uch Sharif Sufi complex
description: Major construction of the tomb complex of Bibi Jawindi and expansion of shrines in Uch Sharif, Punjab, was undertaken. The site became one of the most important Sufi pilgrimage centers in the Indian subcontinent.
outcome: Uch became a major center of Sufi devotion with distinctive architectural style.

title: Kanem Empire's adoption of Islam under Daud Niriri
description: The Kanem Empire in central Sudan saw significant Islamic consolidation under Mai Daud Niriri, who strengthened ties with Mamluk Egypt and promoted Islamic law. This marked a deeper integration of Islam into the Kanem state structure.
sources: Ibn Khaldun, Kitab al-Ibar; Mamluk diplomatic records
outcome: Islam became more deeply rooted in Kanem's political and social institutions.

title: Death of the Sufi master Baha-ud-Din Naqshband
description: Baha-ud-Din Naqshband, founder of the Naqshbandi Sufi order, died in Bukhara. His emphasis on silent dhikr and integration of Sufism with Islamic law created one of the most influential Sufi orders in Islamic history.
outcome: The Naqshbandi order spread across Central Asia, South Asia, and the Ottoman Empire.

title: Foundation of the Samudra Pasai Islamic university
description: The Sultanate of Samudra Pasai in northern Sumatra established a major center of Islamic learning that attracted scholars from across the Muslim world. The institution taught Islamic jurisprudence, theology, and Arabic language.
outcome: Samudra Pasai became the primary center for Islamic education in Southeast Asia.

title: Completion of the Shah-i-Zinda necropolis in Samarkand
description: Major additions to the Shah-i-Zinda mausoleum complex in Samarkand were completed under Timur's patronage. The complex featured stunning tilework and became one of Central Asia's most important pilgrimage sites.
sources: Ruy Gonzalez de Clavijo, Embassy to Tamerlane
outcome: The necropolis exemplified Timurid architectural brilliance and remains a major monument.

title: Battle of Ankara
description: Timur defeated the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I in a decisive battle near Ankara. Bayezid was captured and died in captivity, causing a decade-long Ottoman interregnum.
outcome: The Ottoman state nearly collapsed, delaying its expansion for a generation.

title: Conquest of Ceuta by Portugal
description: Portuguese forces under Prince Henry captured the Moroccan city of Ceuta from the Marinid dynasty. This marked the beginning of European colonial expansion into Muslim North Africa.
sources: Zurara, Cronica da Tomada de Ceuta
outcome: Ceuta became a permanent Portuguese enclave and launched the Age of Exploration.

title: Completion of the Ulugh Beg Observatory
description: Ulugh Beg, the Timurid ruler and astronomer, completed construction of his massive observatory in Samarkand. The observatory produced the Zij-i Sultani, the most accurate star catalogue of its time.
sources: Edward Kennedy, A Survey of Islamic Astronomical Tables
outcome: The observatory advanced astronomical precision and influenced later European astronomy.

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Qasim al-Hadrami
description: The influential Shafi'i scholar and Sufi Ahmad ibn Qasim al-Hadrami died in Yemen. He had established important religious schools and strengthened Islamic learning in Hadramawt.
outcome: His scholarly tradition shaped Yemeni and Indian Ocean Islamic scholarship for centuries.

title: Death of Sultan Ahmad Shah I of Gujarat
description: Ahmad Shah I, founder of Ahmedabad and consolidator of the Gujarat Sultanate, died after a successful reign. He had made Gujarat a major center of Indo-Islamic culture and commerce.
outcome: Gujarat became one of the wealthiest and most culturally vibrant sultanates in India.

title: Barsbay's Conquest of Cyprus
description: Mamluk Sultan Barsbay launched a naval campaign that conquered Cyprus from the Lusignan dynasty. King Janus of Cyprus was captured and forced to pay tribute to Cairo.
outcome: Cyprus became a Mamluk vassal state, strengthening Egyptian control of eastern Mediterranean trade.

title: Battle of Varna
description: Ottoman Sultan Murad II defeated a Crusader army led by King Władysław III of Poland and Hungary. The young king was killed in battle, ending the last major crusade against the Ottomans.
outcome: Ottoman dominance in the Balkans was secured for centuries.

title: Second Battle of Kosovo
description: Ottoman forces under Murad II defeated a Hungarian-led coalition commanded by John Hunyadi. The three-day battle confirmed Ottoman control over the Balkans.
outcome: The Ottoman Empire's European territories were consolidated permanently.

title: Death of Sayyid Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi
description: The influential Maliki jurist and author of important legal commentaries died in Tlemcen. His works became standard references in North African Islamic jurisprudence.
outcome: His legal methodology shaped Maghribi fiqh for generations.

title: Completion of the Gur-e Amir Restoration
description: Major restoration work was completed on the Gur-e Amir mausoleum in Samarkand under Ulugh Beg. The tomb of Timur became a masterpiece of Timurid architecture with its distinctive turquoise dome.
sources: Lisa Golombek, The Timurid Architecture of Iran and Turan
outcome: The monument influenced architectural styles across the Islamic world and became a prototype for Mughal tombs.

title: Siege of Belgrade
description: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II failed to capture Belgrade from Hungarian forces led by John Hunyadi and Giovanni da Capistrano. The defeat temporarily halted Ottoman expansion into Central Europe.
sources: Kritovoulos, History of Mehmed the Conqueror
outcome: Hungarian resistance delayed Ottoman conquest of the region by seventy years.

title: Death of Ulugh Beg
description: The Timurid ruler and astronomer Ulugh Beg was assassinated on the orders of his own son Abd al-Latif. His death marked the decline of scientific patronage in Central Asia.
outcome: The assassination ended the golden age of Timurid science and astronomy.

title: Ottoman Conquest of Trebizond
description: Mehmed II conquered the Empire of Trebizond, the last Greek Byzantine successor state, ending the David Komnenos dynasty. This completed Ottoman control of Anatolia's Black Sea coast.
outcome: The final Byzantine remnant was absorbed into the Ottoman Empire.

title: Establishment of Malacca Sultanate's Golden Age
description: Sultan Mansur Shah ascended the throne of Malacca and expanded its territorial control over the Malay Peninsula and Sumatra. His reign marked the height of Malacca's power as a major Islamic trading empire.
outcome: Malacca became the primary center for Islamic diffusion in Southeast Asia.

title: Death of Uzun Hasan
description: Uzun Hasan, ruler of the Aq Qoyunlu confederation, died after building a powerful state in Iran and eastern Anatolia. His court was a major patron of Persian arts and culture.
outcome: His dynasty's decline paved the way for the rise of the Safavids.

title: Marriage of Ferdinand and Isabella
description: The marriage of Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile united Christian Spain's major kingdoms. This alliance would lead to the final conquest of Granada and end of Muslim rule in Iberia.
sources: Hernando del Pulgar, Crónica de los Reyes Católicos
outcome: The unified Spanish monarchy initiated the final phase of the Reconquista.

title: Founding of the Songhay Empire's Capital at Gao
description: Sunni Ali established Gao as the capital of the expanding Songhay Empire in West Africa. He began transforming Songhay into the dominant power of the Niger River region.
outcome: Songhay became West Africa's largest empire and a major center of Islamic learning.

title: Battle of Otlukbeli
description: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II defeated Uzun Hasan of the Aq Qoyunlu confederation in eastern Anatolia. Ottoman firearms proved decisive against the traditional cavalry tactics of the Turcoman confederation.
outcome: Ottoman supremacy in Anatolia was secured and the Aq Qoyunlu threat ended.

title: Completion of the Topkapi Palace
