description: Mehmed II completed construction of the Topkapi Palace in Istanbul as the primary residence of Ottoman sultans. The palace complex became the administrative and cultural center of the empire.
sources: Gülru Necipoğlu, Architecture, Ceremonial, and Power: The Topkapi Palace
outcome: Topkapi remained the seat of Ottoman power for nearly four centuries.

title: Conquest of Timbuktu by Sunni Ali
description: Sunni Ali of Songhay captured Timbuktu from the Tuareg, incorporating the great center of Islamic learning into his empire. The conquest gave Songhay control over trans-Saharan trade routes.
outcome: Timbuktu's scholars and trade enriched the Songhay Empire.

title: Construction of the Gawhar Shad Mausoleum Complex
description: The mausoleum complex honoring Gawhar Shad, wife of Shah Rukh, was completed in Herat. The structure exemplified Timurid architectural achievement with its intricate tilework and grand scale.
sources: Lisa Golombek, The Timurid Architecture of Iran and Turan
outcome: The complex influenced later Persian and Mughal architectural styles.

title: Death of Jami
description: The celebrated Persian poet and Sufi scholar Abd al-Rahman Jami died in Herat. His works synthesized poetry, mysticism, and philosophy, influencing Persian literary culture for centuries.
outcome: Jami became one of the most celebrated figures in Persian Sufi literature.

title: Bartolomeu Dias Rounds the Cape of Good Hope
description: Portuguese explorer Bartolomeu Dias sailed around the southern tip of Africa, opening a sea route to the Indian Ocean. This threatened Muslim control of Indian Ocean trade networks.
sources: João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia
outcome: Portuguese maritime expansion began to challenge Muslim dominance of trade routes to Asia.

title: Askia Muhammad Usurps Songhay Throne
description: Muhammad Ture overthrew the Sonni dynasty and established the Askia dynasty in Songhay. He strengthened Islamic institutions and made a famous pilgrimage to Mecca.
outcome: The Askia dynasty brought greater Islamic orthodoxy and administrative sophistication to Songhay.

title: Death of Sayyid Muhammad Husayni Gisudaraz
description: The influential Chishti Sufi master known as Gisudaraz died in Gulbarga after decades of teaching. His shrine became a major pilgrimage site in the Deccan region of India.
outcome: His Chishti teachings spread throughout the Deccan and shaped South Indian Islamic spirituality.

title: Death of Bayezid I in Captivity
description: Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I, known as Yıldırım (the Thunderbolt), died in captivity following his capture by Timur at the Battle of Ankara. His death marked the beginning of the Ottoman Interregnum, a period of civil war among his sons that lasted until 1413.
outcome: His death triggered the Ottoman Interregnum, weakening Ottoman power for over a decade.

title: Death of Shah Rukh's Rival Brother Khalil Sultan
description: Khalil Sultan, grandson of Timur who briefly ruled Transoxiana, was defeated and captured by Shah Rukh in the succession struggle for the Timurid Empire. His defeat consolidated Shah Rukh's control over the eastern Timurid domains and allowed for the cultural renaissance in Herat.
outcome: Shah Rukh's victory established Herat as the cultural capital of the Timurid realm for the next four decades.

title: Founding of the Mamluk Naval Arsenal at Alexandria
description: Sultan al-Nasir Faraj ordered the expansion of Alexandria's naval facilities to counter increasing threats from European naval powers to Mamluk trade routes. This investment reflected Mamluk concerns about protecting Red Sea commerce and pilgrimage routes.
outcome: The arsenal strengthened Mamluk naval capabilities during a period of growing European maritime expansion.

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Maqrizi Begins Historical Work
description: The great Mamluk historian al-Maqrizi began his major historical and topographical works in Cairo during this period, including his famous Khitat. His works would become foundational sources for understanding Mamluk Egypt and Islamic urban history.
outcome: Al-Maqrizi's historical writings preserved invaluable information about medieval Islamic civilization.

title: Mehmed I Reunifies the Ottoman State
description: After defeating his brother Musa Çelebi, Mehmed I ended the Ottoman Interregnum and reunified the empire. His victory at the Battle of Çamurlu restored centralized Ottoman authority after more than a decade of civil war.
outcome: The reunification allowed the Ottomans to resume expansion and rebuild their administrative structures.

title: Death of Timur's Daughter Gawhar Shad Becomes Regent
description: Gawhar Shad, wife of Shah Rukh and daughter of a Chagatai noble, began exercising significant political influence in Herat. She became a major patron of architecture and wielded considerable power in Timurid politics for nearly four decades.
outcome: Gawhar Shad's patronage transformed Herat into one of the Islamic world's great cultural centers.

title: Completion of the Gawhar Shad Mosque in Mashhad
description: Gawhar Shad commissioned and completed a magnificent mosque complex adjacent to the shrine of Imam Reza in Mashhad. The mosque, designed by architect Qavam al-Din Shirazi, exemplified the refinement of Timurid architectural style.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: The mosque became one of the masterpieces of Timurid architecture and enhanced Mashhad's status as a pilgrimage center.

title: Death of Sultan Ahmad of the Bahmani Sultanate
description: Ahmad Shah I Wali of the Bahmani Sultanate died after establishing the new capital at Bidar and expanding Bahmani power in the Deccan. His reign saw increased Persian cultural influence and architectural development in the Deccan sultanates.
outcome: His death led to succession struggles that eventually weakened the unified Bahmani state.

title: Death of Ahmad al-Qalqashandi
description: Ahmad al-Qalqashandi, the renowned Mamluk encyclopedist and author of the administrative manual Subh al-A'sha, died in Cairo. His fourteen-volume work preserved detailed knowledge of chancery practice, geography, and administrative organization.
outcome: His encyclopedic work remained an essential reference for understanding Mamluk administration and medieval Islamic bureaucracy.

title: Establishment of the Malindi-Kilwa Trade Alliance
description: The Swahili city-states of Malindi and Kilwa formalized commercial relationships that strengthened East African trade networks. These Muslim-ruled ports dominated Indian Ocean commerce, trading gold, ivory, and slaves with Arabia, Persia, and India.
outcome: The alliance strengthened Swahili commercial dominance before Portuguese disruption in the next century.

title: Death of Qadi Siraj al-Din al-Bulqini
description: Siraj al-Din al-Bulqini, one of the most distinguished Shafi'i jurists and hadith scholars of the Mamluk period, died in Cairo. He served as chief judge of Egypt and authored numerous works on Islamic jurisprudence and hadith methodology.
outcome: His legal opinions and methodological works influenced Shafi'i jurisprudence for generations.

title: Death of Emperor Yeshaq of Ethiopia After Muslim Conflicts
description: Ethiopian Emperor Yeshaq I died after decades of warfare with the Muslim sultanate of Adal and other Islamic polities on Ethiopia's eastern borders. His reign saw intensified Christian-Muslim conflict in the Horn of Africa.
sources: Ethiopian royal chronicles; Arab chronicles of Adal
outcome: The conflicts established patterns of Christian-Muslim rivalry that would intensify in the following century.

title: Establishment of the Delhi Sultanate's Sayyid Dynasty Capital Works
description: Sultan Muhammad Shah of the Sayyid dynasty undertook significant restoration of Delhi's monuments and infrastructure despite the dynasty's weakened state. The Sayyids struggled to maintain control over a fragmenting Delhi Sultanate.
sources: Yahya ibn Ahmad Sirhindi, Tarikh-i Mubarak Shahi
outcome: Despite architectural efforts, the Sayyid dynasty's weakness paved the way for the Lodi dynasty's rise.

title: Death of al-Maqrizi
description: The great Mamluk historian Taqi al-Din al-Maqrizi died in Cairo after producing monumental historical and topographical works. His Khitat remains an invaluable source for understanding medieval Cairo and Islamic urban civilization.
outcome: His historical methodology and comprehensive documentation influenced Islamic historiography for centuries.

title: Accession of Mehmed II
description: Mehmed II became Ottoman Sultan for the second time following his father Murad II's death. The young sultan immediately began preparations for the conquest of Constantinople, which he viewed as essential to Ottoman legitimacy.
sources: Kritovoulos, History of Mehmed the Conqueror
outcome: His accession marked the beginning of aggressive Ottoman expansion that would transform the empire into a world power.

title: Death of Parameswara's Dynasty in Malacca
description: The Malacca Sultanate under Sultan Muzaffar Shah consolidated its position as the dominant Muslim trading power in Southeast Asia. The sultanate controlled the strategic Malacca Strait and actively spread Islam throughout the Malay archipelago.
outcome: Malacca's commercial success made it a model for Islamic state-building in Southeast Asia.

title: Foundation of the Hafsid Library at Tunis
description: Sultan Abu Amr Uthman of the Hafsid dynasty established a major library and madrasa complex in Tunis, collecting manuscripts from across the Islamic world. The institution became a center for North African scholarship during the dynasty's late period.
sources: Ibn Abi Dinar, al-Mu'nis fi Akhbar Ifriqiya wa Tunis
outcome: The library preserved important texts and sustained scholarly activity in Hafsid Tunisia.

title: Ottoman Conquest of Trebizond's Preparation
description: Mehmed II began diplomatic and military pressure on the Empire of Trebizond, the last Byzantine successor state, which would fall in 1461. The conquest eliminated the final Greek Christian state on Anatolian soil.
sources: Kritovoulos, History of Mehmed the Conqueror
outcome: Trebizond's fall completed Ottoman control of Anatolian coastlines and ended Byzantine political existence.

title: Death of Sayyid Muhammad al-Jazuli
description: Muhammad ibn Sulayman al-Jazuli, founder of the Jazuliyya Sufi order in Morocco, died after establishing a major movement emphasizing devotion to the Prophet Muhammad. His teachings sparked a Sufi revival in the Maghrib and influenced Moroccan religious life for centuries.
outcome: The Jazuliyya order became a major force in Moroccan Islam and anti-Portuguese resistance.

title: Establishment of the Aq Qoyunlu Capital at Tabriz
description: Uzun Hasan of the Aq Qoyunlu confederation made Tabriz his capital after defeating the Qara Qoyunlu and establishing control over much of Persia and Iraq. His court attracted scholars and artists, making Tabriz a major cultural center.
outcome: Tabriz flourished as a center of Persian culture and became a bridge between Timurid and Safavid periods.

title: Death of Ali al-Qushji in Constantinople
description: Ali al-Qushji, the renowned astronomer and mathematician who worked at Ulugh Beg's observatory, died in Constantinople after being invited by Mehmed II. He brought Central Asian astronomical and mathematical knowledge to the Ottoman Empire.
outcome: His work established a tradition of mathematical astronomy in the Ottoman Empire.

title: Ottoman Conquest of Crimea
description: Mehmed II brought the Crimean Khanate under Ottoman suzerainty after military intervention, establishing the Giray dynasty as Ottoman vassals. This extended Ottoman influence into the Black Sea region and established a lasting relationship with Crimean Tatars.
outcome: Ottoman-Crimean alliance became crucial for Ottoman power projection in Eastern Europe and the steppes.

title: Death of Mehmed II the Conqueror
description: Sultan Mehmed II died during a military campaign, ending a transformative reign that saw the conquest of Constantinople, expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans, and patronage of arts and sciences. His death triggered a succession crisis between his sons Bayezid and Cem.
outcome: His death marked the end of an era of rapid Ottoman expansion and cultural synthesis.

title: Establishment of the Lodi Dynasty's Final Phase
description: Sikandar Lodi became Sultan of Delhi, moving the capital from Delhi to Agra and attempting to reassert central authority over the fragmenting Delhi Sultanate. His reign saw administrative reforms but continued decline of sultanic power.
sources: Abbas Khan Sarwani, Tarikh-i Sher Shahi
outcome: Despite reforms, the Lodi dynasty's weakness set the stage for Babur's invasion and Mughal conquest.

title: Publication of al-Suyuti's al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Qur'an
description: Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti completed his comprehensive manual of Qur'anic sciences in Cairo, synthesizing centuries of Islamic scholarship on Qur'anic interpretation and sciences. The work became one of the most influential texts in Islamic studies.
outcome: Al-Itqan remained a standard reference for Qur'anic studies throughout the Islamic world.

title: Death of Baba Battikh in Tlemcen
description: The North African Sufi saint Baba Battikh died in Tlemcen after establishing a zawiya that became an important center for Islamic learning in western Algeria. His teachings emphasized asceticism and service to the community.
outcome: His zawiya continued as a center of spiritual life in Tlemcen and resisted Portuguese and Spanish pressures.

title: Death of Ibn Arafa in Tunis
description: Muhammad ibn Arafa al-Warghammi, renowned Maliki jurist and mufti of Tunis, died after decades of teaching and issuing fatwas. He was considered one of the most influential North African scholars of his generation, authoring important commentaries on Maliki jurisprudence.
outcome: His legal opinions and commentaries remained authoritative in North African Maliki scholarship for centuries.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad Shah I of Gujarat
description: Muhammad Shah I, founder of the independent Gujarat Sultanate who broke away from the Delhi Sultanate, died after establishing Ahmadabad as his capital. His reign marked the beginning of Gujarat's rise as a major commercial and political power in western India.
outcome: Gujarat became a major independent sultanate that would dominate western Indian Ocean trade for the next century.

title: Death of al-Firuzabadi
description: Muhammad ibn Ya'qub al-Firuzabadi, compiler of the influential Arabic dictionary al-Qamus al-Muhit, died in Zabid, Yemen. His lexicographical work became one of the most widely used Arabic dictionaries in the Islamic world.
outcome: Al-Qamus al-Muhit remained a standard reference for Arabic lexicography for centuries.

title: Death of Ali ibn Muhammad al-Jurjani
description: Al-Sayyid al-Sharif al-Jurjani, major scholar of Islamic theology, philosophy, and Arabic grammar, died in Shiraz. He authored influential works including commentaries on al-Taftazani's writings and the famous Kitab al-Ta'rifat (Book of Definitions).
outcome: His philosophical and theological works became standard texts in madrasas across the Islamic world.

title: Establishment of the Rasulid Observatory at Ta'izz
description: Sultan al-Ashraf Isma'il II of the Rasulid dynasty established an astronomical observatory in Ta'izz, Yemen, building on the dynasty's tradition of scientific patronage. The sultan himself was an accomplished astronomer who compiled astronomical tables.
sources: King, The Ciphers of the Monks
Varisco, Medieval Agriculture and Islamic Science
outcome: The observatory contributed to Yemen's reputation as a center of astronomical research in the 15th century.

title: Death of Qadi Iyad's Successor Ahmad al-Qastallani
description: Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Qastallani, Maliki scholar and commentator on Islamic law, died in Tunis. He was known for his expertise in hadith studies and legal theory within the North African scholarly tradition.
outcome: His works contributed to the continuation of Maliki jurisprudence in the Maghrib.

title: Death of Sultan Barsbay's Naval Commander
description: The death of a key Mamluk naval commander marked a transition in Mediterranean naval power during Sultan Barsbay's reign. Mamluk naval forces had been actively engaged in combating European pirates and protecting Red Sea trade routes.
outcome: Mamluk naval policy shifted focus toward monopolizing spice trade rather than Mediterranean expansion.

title: Death of Yusuf III of Granada
description: Yusuf III, Nasrid sultan of Granada who managed to maintain relative stability during a period of Castilian pressure, died after a reign marked by diplomatic maneuvering. His death led to renewed internal conflicts within the last Muslim kingdom in Iberia.
outcome: Granada's internal instability accelerated, weakening the sultanate against Christian pressure.

title: Foundation of the Qara Qoyunlu Capital at Tabriz
description: Jahan Shah of the Qara Qoyunlu (Black Sheep) Turkmen confederation established Tabriz as his capital, beginning a period of architectural and cultural patronage. The Qara Qoyunlu controlled much of Azerbaijan, Iraq, and western Iran.
sources: Minorsky, La Perse au XVe siècle
outcome: Tabriz became a major center of Turkmen power and Persian culture before the Aq Qoyunlu conquest.

title: Death of Sayyid Khizr Khan of Delhi
description: Khizr Khan, founder of the Sayyid Dynasty of Delhi and former governor under Timur, died after establishing a fragile sultanate. Though claiming Sayyid descent, his dynasty ruled a much-reduced Delhi Sultanate surrounded by independent Muslim kingdoms.
sources: Yahya ibn Ahmad Sirhindi, Tarikh-i Mubarak Shahi
outcome: The Sayyid Dynasty's weakness allowed regional sultanates to consolidate independence from Delhi.

title: Battle of Anatolian Unification Under Murad II
description: Sultan Murad II defeated the final Anatolian beyliks resisting Ottoman authority, consolidating control over western and central Anatolia. The campaigns included the subjugation of Karaman and other Turkish principalities.
outcome: Ottoman control of Anatolia was consolidated, preparing the empire for expansion into the Balkans and eastward.

title: Death of Ibrahim Shah of Jaunpur
description: Ibrahim Shah Sharqi, sultan of Jaunpur Sultanate in northern India and patron of Indo-Islamic architecture, died after a reign that saw Jaunpur rival Delhi culturally. His court attracted scholars and artists from across the Islamic world.
sources: Abbas Khan Sarwani, Tarikh-i Sher Shahi
outcome: Jaunpur's distinctive architectural style flourished, producing monuments that blended Persian and Indian elements.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad Shah II Bahmani
description: Muhammad Shah II Bahmani, ruler of the Bahmani Sultanate in the Deccan, died after a reign marked by conflicts with Vijayanagara and internal factional strife. His reign saw increased Persian cultural influence at the Bahmani court.
outcome: The Bahmani Sultanate's fragmentation into regional kingdoms accelerated after his death.

title: Death of Abu al-Hasan al-Jazuli
description: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Sulayman al-Jazuli, founder of the Jazuliyya Sufi order in Morocco, died after decades of teaching and spiritual guidance. His order emphasized devotion to the Prophet Muhammad and would become highly influential in North and West Africa.
outcome: The Jazuliyya tariqa became the dominant Sufi order in Morocco and spread to West Africa.

title: Construction of the Fatih Mosque Complex Begins
description: Sultan Mehmed II ordered construction of the Fatih Mosque complex in Constantinople following the conquest, commissioning architect Atik Sinan. The complex included a mosque, madrasas, hospital, and public kitchen, symbolizing the city's transformation.
sources: Goodwin, A History of Ottoman Architecture
outcome: The Fatih complex established the model for large Ottoman imperial mosque complexes.

title: Reign of Sultan Mahmud Khalji in Malwa
description: Mahmud Khalji I consolidated power in the Malwa Sultanate in central India, beginning an era of military expansion and cultural patronage. His reign saw Malwa emerge as a major power challenging both Gujarat and Delhi.
outcome: Malwa became a significant regional power and center of Indo-Islamic culture in central India.

title: Ottoman Conquest of Sinope
description: Mehmed II conquered Sinope, the last independent Turkmen state on the Black Sea coast, ending the Isfendiyarid beylik. The conquest completed Ottoman control of the Anatolian Black Sea coastline.
sources: Kritovoulos, History of Mehmed the Conqueror
outcome: Ottoman control of Black Sea trade routes was secured, isolating remaining independent states.

title: Death of Sonni Sulayman Dandi of Songhay
description: Sonni Sulayman Dandi, ruler of the Songhay Empire, died and was succeeded by Sonni Ali, who would transform Songhay into a major imperial power. The succession marked the beginning of Songhay's golden age.
outcome: Sonni Ali's accession initiated military campaigns that would create West Africa's largest empire.

title: Death of Skanderbeg and Ottoman Return to Albania
description: The death of Albanian resistance leader Skanderbeg removed the main obstacle to Ottoman control of Albania. Sultan Mehmed II's forces quickly moved to consolidate Ottoman authority over Albanian territories.
sources: Barletius, Historia de vita et gestis Scanderbegi
outcome: Albania was fully integrated into the Ottoman Empire, ending decades of resistance.

title: Portuguese Conquest of Tangier
description: Portuguese forces under Afonso V captured Tangier from the Wattasid dynasty of Morocco, expanding Portuguese control in North Africa. The conquest was part of Portugal's strategy to control Mediterranean trade and combat Muslim powers.
sources: de Zurara, Chronica do Conde D. Pedro de Menezes
outcome: Portuguese expansion in Morocco prompted increased resistance and eventual Saadian rise to power.

title: Death of Ali Qushji's Astronomical Work in Istanbul
description: Following the death of astronomer Ali Qushji in 1474, his students completed and disseminated his major astronomical treatise in Istanbul. Qushji had brought Samarqand's astronomical knowledge to the Ottoman court.
sources: İhsanoğlu, Science, Technology and Learning in the Ottoman Empire
outcome: Ottoman astronomy integrated the Samarqand tradition, establishing Istanbul as a center of astronomical research.

title: Treaty of Constantinople Between Venice and Ottomans
description: The Ottoman Empire and Venice signed a peace treaty ending the Ottoman-Venetian War of 1463-1479. Venice ceded key territories including Negroponte and paid an annual tribute for trading privileges.
sources: Setton, The Papacy and the Levant
İnalcık, An Economic and Social History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: The treaty confirmed Ottoman dominance in the eastern Mediterranean and Aegean.

title: Boabdil Becomes Sultan of Granada
description: Abu Abdullah Muhammad XII, known as Boabdil, seized the throne of Granada from his father Abu al-Hasan Ali, sparking civil war within the last Muslim kingdom in Iberia. The internal conflict fatally weakened Granada against Castilian pressure.
sources: Washington Irving, Chronicle of the Conquest of Granada
outcome: Granada's civil war enabled Ferdinand and Isabella to exploit divisions and accelerate the Reconquista.

title: Construction of the Gök Medrese in Sivas
description: The Gök Medrese, an important theological school featuring distinctive Seljuk-Ottoman transitional architecture, was completed in Sivas. The building combined traditional Anatolian architectural elements with Ottoman innovations.
sources: Goodwin, A History of Ottoman Architecture
outcome: The medrese exemplified the architectural synthesis occurring in Anatolia under Ottoman rule.

title: Death of Al-Suyuti Begins Final Works
description: Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, one of Islam's most prolific scholars, entered his final decade of scholarship in Cairo, having already authored hundreds of works. He continued producing major works on Quranic exegesis, hadith, and Islamic sciences until his death in 1505.
outcome: Al-Suyuti's encyclopedic works became foundational references across Islamic disciplines for centuries.

title: Death of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi's Successor Qadi Zadeh al-Rumi
description: Qadi Zadeh al-Rumi, Ottoman mathematician and astronomer who wrote commentaries on works by al-Tusi and Ulugh Beg's teacher, died in Bursa. His works on mathematics and astronomy formed the foundation for the Samarkand school of astronomy.
sources: Seyyed Hossein Nasr, Islamic Science: An Illustrated Study
outcome: His commentaries became standard texts in Ottoman madrasas for centuries.

title: Death of Sultan Muzaffar I of Gujarat
description: Sultan Muzaffar Shah I, founder of the independent Gujarat Sultanate after breaking from the Delhi Sultanate, died after establishing Ahmedabad as a major center. His reign marked the beginning of Gujarat's rise as a major maritime trading power.
sources: R.C. Majumdar, The History and Culture of the Indian People
outcome: His dynasty ruled Gujarat for nearly two centuries, transforming it into a major trading hub.

title: Death of Sultan Hasan Shah of Kashmir
description: Sultan Hasan Shah of Kashmir died after a reign marked by administrative reforms and the expansion of Islamic institutions in the Kashmir Valley. He established Persian as the court language and promoted Sufi orders.
sources: Mohibbul Hasan, Kashmir Under the Sultans
outcome: His policies strengthened Persian cultural influence in Kashmir.

title: Death of Hafiz Abru, Timurid Court Historian
description: Hafiz-i Abru, official historian of the Timurid court who wrote comprehensive histories including Zubdat al-Tawarikh, died in Herat. His geographical and historical works documented Timur's campaigns and the early Timurid period.
sources: John E. Woods, The Timurid Dynasty
outcome: His chronicles remain primary sources for Timurid history and geography.

title: Death of Sultan Yusuf II of Granada
description: Sultan Yusuf II of the Nasrid dynasty died after a reign marked by internal conflicts and pressure from Castile. His death led to further dynastic struggles that weakened Granada's ability to resist Christian advances.
outcome: His troubled reign accelerated Granada's decline in the face of Christian expansion.

title: Establishment of the Sultanate of Kilwa's New Palace Complex
description: Sultan al-Hasan ibn Sulaiman completed the great palace at Husuni Kubwa in Kilwa, showcasing the wealth generated by Indian Ocean trade. The complex featured elaborate courtyards, bathing pools, and trading facilities.
sources: Chapurukha Kusimba, The Rise and Fall of Swahili States
outcome: The palace demonstrated Kilwa's prominence as a major Swahili trading center.

title: Death of Sayyid Ala al-Din Ali of the Delhi Sayyid Dynasty
description: Ala al-Din Alam Shah, last sultan of the Sayyid Dynasty of Delhi, was deposed and died after withdrawing to Badaun. His removal by Bahlul Lodi ended the Sayyid Dynasty and established the Lodi Dynasty.
sources: Peter Jackson, The Delhi Sultanate: A Political and Military History
outcome: The transition to Lodi rule marked a new phase in the Delhi Sultanate's history.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad III Bahmani
description: Sultan Muhammad Shah III of the Bahmani Sultanate died after a reign marked by conflicts with Vijayanagara and internal ministerial power struggles. His reign saw the increasing influence of Deccan regional powers.
sources: Richard M. Eaton, A Social History of the Deccan
outcome: His death contributed to the gradual fragmentation of the Bahmani state.

title: Death of Sonni Ali's Predecessor Sonni Sulayman
description: Sonni Sulayman Dandi, ruler of the nascent Songhay Empire, died and was succeeded by his more capable son Sonni Ali. His death marked the transition to Songhay's period of major expansion.
sources: Nehemia Levtzion, Ancient Ghana and Mali
outcome: Sonni Ali would transform Songhay into the dominant West African empire.

title: Death of Khoja Ahrar, Naqshbandi Master
description: Khoja Ubaidullah Ahrar, powerful Naqshbandi Sufi master who wielded enormous influence in Timurid Transoxiana, died in Samarkand. He established extensive waqf foundations and shaped political appointments through his spiritual authority.
sources: Devin DeWeese, The Politics of Sacred Lineages in Central Asia
outcome: His legacy established the model of politically engaged Naqshbandi Sufism in Central Asia.

title: Death of Sultan Husayn Bayqara's Rival Abu Sa'id
description: Timurid ruler Abu Sa'id Mirza was defeated and executed by Uzun Hasan of the Aq Qoyunlu after the Battle of Qarabagh. His death ended Timurid control over western Iran and fragmented the empire.
sources: Beatrice Forbes Manz, Power, Politics and Religion in Timurid Iran
outcome: The Timurid realm permanently split between Khorasan and Transoxiana.

title: Accession of Bayezid II After Civil War
description: Bayezid II secured the Ottoman throne after defeating his brother Cem Sultan in a succession struggle following Mehmed II's death. Cem fled to the Mamluks and later Europe, where his presence influenced Ottoman foreign policy.
outcome: Bayezid's reign marked a period of consolidation and cultural flourishing after Mehmed's conquests.

title: Battle of Shonthor
description: Sultan Sikandar Lodi of Delhi defeated the combined forces of the Sharqi dynasty of Jaunpur in a decisive battle near the Ganges. The victory consolidated Lodi control over eastern Uttar Pradesh.
sources: Iqtidar Husain Siddiqui, The Delhi Sultanate: Studies in History
outcome: The battle marked the end of Jaunpur's independence and Lodi consolidation of northern India.

title: Death of Abd al-Rahman al-Tha'alibi
description: Abd al-Rahman al-Tha'alibi, prominent North African Maliki scholar and Quranic commentator, died in Algiers. His tafsir and works on fiqh became widely studied throughout the Maghreb.
sources: Mohamed El Mansour, Morocco in the Reign of Mawlay Sulayman
outcome: His scholarship reinforced Maliki tradition in North Africa during Ottoman expansion.

title: Completion of the Masjid-i Shah in Bidar
description: The congregational mosque in Bidar, commissioned by the Bahmani dynasty with distinctive Persian-Deccan architectural synthesis, was completed. The mosque featured elaborate tile work and calligraphy imported from Persia.
sources: George Michell, Architecture of the Islamic World
outcome: The mosque exemplified the cultural synthesis of the Deccan sultanates.

title: Battle of Sharur — Safavid consolidation in Azerbaijan
description: Shah Ismail I defeats the Aq Qoyunlu forces near Sharur, solidifying Safavid control over Azerbaijan. This victory allowed the young Shah to continue his campaign to establish Twelver Shi'ism as the state religion.
outcome: Enabled Safavid dominance in northwest Iran and the imposition of Shi'ism.

title: Leo Africanus completes travels in West Africa
description: Al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan al-Fasi, later known as Leo Africanus, completes extensive travels through Timbuktu, Gao, and Songhai territories. His observations documented the flourishing Islamic scholarly culture and trans-Saharan trade networks.
outcome: His later writings provided Europeans with detailed knowledge of West African Muslim civilizations.

title: Ottomans conquer Damascus from Mamluks
description: Sultan Selim I enters Damascus after defeating Mamluk forces at Marj Dabiq, ending Mamluk rule in Syria. The city's scholars and artisans were subsequently relocated to enrich Ottoman Istanbul.
outcome: Syria became an Ottoman province for four centuries.

title: Hafsid dynasty faces Spanish attack on Tunis
description: Spanish forces under Charles V briefly occupy the port of La Goulette near Tunis, threatening Hafsid Sultan Muhammad V. Ottoman naval support under Hayreddin Barbarossa would later prove crucial to North African Muslim states.
sources: Robert Brunschvig, La Berbérie Orientale sous les Hafsides
outcome: Demonstrated vulnerability of North African states to European naval power.

title: Babur completes Baburnama memoir
description: Mughal Emperor Babur finishes his autobiography in Chagatai Turkic, detailing his Central Asian origins, conquests in India, and personal reflections. The work is considered a masterpiece of world literature for its frankness and literary quality.
outcome: Established the tradition of Mughal autobiographical writing and provided invaluable historical documentation.

title: Ottomans conquer Baghdad from Safavids
description: Sultan Suleiman I captures Baghdad from Safavid control, restoring Sunni rule and Ottoman dominance over Iraq. The conquest secured pilgrimage routes to Mecca and expanded Ottoman territory eastward.
outcome: Iraq remained largely under Ottoman control until the 20th century.

title: Battle of Preveza — Ottoman naval supremacy
description: Admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa defeats a Holy League fleet off the coast of Preveza in western Greece. The Ottoman victory secured Mediterranean dominance for decades.
outcome: Confirmed Ottoman naval supremacy in the eastern Mediterranean.

title: Sher Shah Suri defeats Humayun at Kannauj
description: Afghan ruler Sher Shah Suri decisively defeats Mughal Emperor Humayun at the Battle of Kannauj, forcing him into fifteen years of exile. Sher Shah established efficient administration and built the Grand Trunk Road across North India.
sources: Abbas Khan Sarwani, Tarikh-i Sher Shahi
outcome: Temporarily interrupted Mughal rule and reformed North Indian administration.

title: Ottomans and French alliance against Habsburgs
description: Sultan Suleiman I and King Francis I of France formalize their alliance, with joint operations in the Mediterranean including the siege of Nice. This unprecedented Muslim-Christian alliance shocked Catholic Europe.
sources: Christine Isom-Verhaaren, Allies with the Infidel
outcome: Established precedent for Ottoman-European diplomatic and military cooperation.

title: Death of mathematician al-Qalasadi in Tunisia
description: Abu'l-Hasan ibn Ali al-Qalasadi, Andalusian mathematician who pioneered algebraic symbolism, dies in Béja. His works systematized mathematical notation used across the Muslim West.
outcome: His symbolic methods influenced later European algebraic notation.

title: Peace of Amasya between Ottomans and Safavids
description: Sultan Suleiman I and Shah Tahmasp I sign a treaty ending decades of warfare, establishing borders and recognizing Ottoman control of Iraq and Safavid control of Azerbaijan. The agreement brought temporary stability to the region.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Established Ottoman-Safavid border that lasted with modifications into the 20th century.

title: Second Battle of Panipat — Akbar consolidates power
description: The young Mughal Emperor Akbar, led by regent Bairam Khan, defeats the Hindu general Hemu at Panipat. The victory secured Mughal rule over northern India after Humayun's recent restoration.
outcome: Firmly established Mughal dominance in North India for the next century.

title: Akbar abolishes jizya tax on pilgrims
description: Emperor Akbar eliminates the jizya tax on Hindu pilgrims visiting holy sites, beginning his policy of religious tolerance. This marked the first of several reforms aimed at integrating Hindu subjects into the Mughal state.
outcome: Initiated Akbar's distinctive policy of sulh-i kul (universal peace) that defined his reign.

title: Battle of Talikota destroys Vijayanagara Empire
description: Coalition of Deccan Sultanates defeats the Hindu Vijayanagara Empire at Talikota, ending centuries of Muslim-Hindu balance in South India. The Adil Shahi, Qutb Shahi, Nizam Shahi, and Bijapur sultanates cooperated in this decisive victory.
sources: Richard Eaton, A Social History of the Deccan
outcome: Established Muslim political supremacy in the Deccan plateau.

title: Akbar conquers Ranthambore Fort
description: Mughal forces under Akbar capture the strategic Ranthambore Fort in Rajasthan after a prolonged siege. The conquest was part of Akbar's systematic subjugation of Rajput kingdoms.
outcome: Extended Mughal control over Rajputana through combination of conquest and marriage alliances.

title: Akbar establishes Fatehpur Sikri as capital
description: Emperor Akbar founds Fatehpur Sikri near Agra, building a magnificent new capital with palaces, mosques, and courtyards blending Persian, Indian, and Islamic architectural styles. The Buland Darwaza gateway commemorated his conquest of Gujarat.
outcome: Represented the zenith of Akbar's architectural patronage and syncretic cultural vision.

title: Ottomans conquer Tunis from Hafsids
description: Ottoman forces under Sinan Pasha capture Tunis, ending the Hafsid dynasty that had ruled Tunisia since 1229. The conquest completed Ottoman control over North Africa except Morocco.
outcome: Extended Ottoman authority across the Maghreb and secured Mediterranean trade routes.

title: Akbar establishes Ibadat Khana for religious debates
description: Emperor Akbar founds the Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) at Fatehpur Sikri for interfaith theological discussions among Muslims, Hindus, Christians, Zoroastrians, and Jains. Initially restricted to Muslim scholars, it was later opened to all faiths.
outcome: Laid groundwork for Akbar's Din-i Ilahi and his eclectic religious policies.

title: Battle of Alcácer Quibir — Portuguese disaster in Morocco
description: Sultan Abd al-Malik al-Saadi defeats Portuguese King Sebastian I at Alcácer Quibir in Morocco, with all three rival claimants dying during the battle. The Portuguese defeat ended their North African ambitions and led to Spanish annexation of Portugal.
sources: Weston Cook, The Hundred Years War for Morocco
outcome: Preserved Moroccan independence and devastated Portuguese royal finances and prestige.

title: Ottoman architect Sinan completes Sokollu Mehmed Pasha Mosque
description: Mimar Sinan, chief Ottoman architect at age ninety, completes the Sokollu Mehmed Pasha Mosque in Istanbul's Kadırga district. The mosque exemplifies his mature style with innovative use of space and light.
sources: Aptullah Kuran, Sinan: The Grand Old Master of Ottoman Architecture
outcome: Demonstrated Sinan's enduring creativity in his final decade of work.

title: Akbar promulgates Din-i Ilahi religious synthesis
description: Emperor Akbar formally establishes Din-i Ilahi (Religion of God), a syncretic faith drawing on Islam, Hinduism, Zoroastrianism, and Christianity emphasizing reason and imperial authority. Only a small circle of courtiers adopted the teachings.
outcome: Provoked orthodox Muslim opposition but reflected Akbar's attempt to transcend sectarian divisions.

title: Akbar conquers Kashmir
description: Mughal forces under Raja Bhagwan Das capture Kashmir from the independent Chak dynasty, incorporating the strategic Himalayan region into the empire. Kashmir became a favored summer retreat for Mughal emperors.
outcome: Extended Mughal control to the northern frontiers and secured a strategic buffer region.

title: Treaty of Istanbul — Ottomans gain Caucasus territories
description: Sultan Murad III and Safavid Shah Abbas I sign a treaty ending the Ottoman-Safavid War, granting Ottomans control over Georgia, Azerbaijan, and parts of the Caucasus. The treaty freed Shah Abbas to focus on Uzbek threats in the east.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Represented the height of Ottoman territorial expansion in the Caucasus.

title: Moroccan conquest of Songhai Empire
description: Saadian Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur sends a military expedition across the Sahara that defeats the Songhai Empire at the Battle of Tondibi using firearms. Moroccan forces occupied Timbuktu and Gao, disrupting trans-Saharan trade networks.
outcome: Fragmented West Africa's largest empire and shifted trade routes, contributing to regional decline.

title: Shah Abbas I relocates Safavid capital to Isfahan
description: Shah Abbas I moves the Safavid capital from Qazvin to Isfahan, beginning massive construction projects that transformed the city. The Naqsh-e Jahan Square and surrounding monuments epitomized Safavid architectural achievement.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Isfahan became one of the world's most beautiful cities and a center of Persian culture.

title: Ottomans capture Durazzo from Venice
description: Ottoman forces under Bayezid II captured the strategic Adriatic port of Durazzo (Durrës) from Venetian control. This consolidated Ottoman dominance over the eastern Adriatic and Albanian coastline.
outcome: Ottoman control over Albanian coast strengthened, limiting Venetian influence in the Adriatic.

title: Battle of Diu — Portuguese naval victory over combined Muslim fleet
description: Portuguese forces under Francisco de Almeida defeated a combined Mamluk-Gujarat-Calicut fleet off Diu. This battle secured Portuguese naval supremacy in the Indian Ocean and disrupted traditional Muslim trade routes.
outcome: Portuguese dominance in Indian Ocean trade established for the century.

title: Death of Askia Muhammad — peak of Songhai Empire
description: Askia Muhammad I, who transformed Songhai into West Africa's largest Islamic empire, was deposed by his son. His reign saw expansion of Islamic learning in Timbuktu and Gao, establishing renowned madrasas.
outcome: Songhai remained the dominant Islamic power in West Africa for another 75 years.

title: Death of Ismail al-Jazuli's successor — spread of Jazuliyya Sufism
description: The death of Muhammad ibn Sulayman al-Jazuli accelerated the spread of the Jazuliyya Sufi order across Morocco and West Africa. The order played a crucial role in both spiritual life and resistance to Portuguese expansion.
outcome: Jazuliyya order became dominant spiritual force in Morocco and influenced anti-colonial resistance.

title: Portuguese establish fortified trade post at Chittagong
description: Portuguese traders established a permanent settlement in Chittagong under permission from the Bengal Sultanate. This became a major center for trade between the Muslim world and Southeast Asia.
sources: João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia
outcome: Chittagong became a crucial link in Indian Ocean trade networks for the next century.

title: Ahmad Gran begins jihad in Ethiopian highlands
description: Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi (Ahmad Gran) launched a major military campaign from Adal Sultanate against Christian Ethiopia. His conquests brought much of the Ethiopian highlands under Muslim control for over a decade.
outcome: Muslim control of Ethiopian highlands lasted until Portuguese intervention in 1543.

title: Death of poet Fuzuli in Baghdad
description: Muhammad bin Suleyman Fuzuli, master of Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic poetry, died in Baghdad. His works, especially his diwan and the tragic romance Leyla and Majnun, profoundly influenced Ottoman and Persian literature.
outcome: Fuzuli's literary style shaped Ottoman poetry for generations and remains celebrated in Turkish literature.

title: Ottomans capture Aden and extend control to Yemen
description: Ottoman admiral Suleiman Pasha captured Aden from local rulers, establishing Ottoman presence in Yemen. This secured control over Red Sea trade routes and countered Portuguese influence in the Indian Ocean.
outcome: Ottoman control of Yemen secured Red Sea trade and projected power toward India.

title: Death of Ahmad Gran ends Muslim conquest of Ethiopia
description: Ahmad Gran was killed in battle at Wayna Daga by Ethiopian and Portuguese forces. His death ended the Adal Sultanate's conquest of Ethiopia and marked the decline of Muslim power in the Horn of Africa.
outcome: Ethiopian Christian kingdom survived and Muslim influence in highlands declined.

title: Kazan Khanate falls to Ivan IV of Russia
description: Russian forces under Ivan the Terrible conquered Kazan, ending the Tatar Muslim khanate that had existed since the Golden Horde's dissolution. Tens of thousands of Muslims were killed or displaced.
outcome: Russian expansion into Muslim territories began, eventually reaching Central Asia.

title: Astrakhan Khanate conquered by Russia
description: Ivan IV captured Astrakhan, the last major Tatar Muslim khanate on the Volga, ending Muslim political independence in the region. This opened the Caspian to Russian control.
outcome: Russian control of Volga route to Caspian established, isolating Central Asian Muslims.

title: Death of scholar Ibn Hajar al-Haytami in Mecca
description: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Hajar al-Haytami, leading Shafi'i jurist and prolific author, died in Mecca. His fatwas and legal works, including al-Zawajir, shaped Islamic jurisprudence across the Shafi'i world.
outcome: His legal opinions remained authoritative references for Shafi'i scholars for centuries.

title: Moriscos revolt in Granada against forced conversions
description: Moriscos (forced Muslim converts) in the Alpujarras mountains rebelled against Spanish prohibitions of Islamic practices. The revolt lasted until 1571 and resulted in mass deportations from Granada.
sources: Diego Hurtado de Mendoza, Guerra de Granada
outcome: Moriscos were expelled from Granada, dispersing across Spain and marking the near-end of Muslim presence in Iberia.

title: Ottomans conquer Cyprus from Venice
description: Ottoman forces under Lala Mustafa Pasha conquered Cyprus after a year-long campaign, ending Venetian rule. The fall of Famagusta was particularly brutal and provoked European outrage.
outcome: Cyprus remained Ottoman for three centuries, becoming a center of Ottoman Mediterranean power.

title: Ottomans recapture Tunis permanently from Spain
description: Ottoman forces under Sinan Pasha definitively recaptured Tunis from Spanish control, ending decades of back-and-forth control. This established Ottoman authority over the Maghreb coast.
outcome: Ottoman suzerainty over North Africa confirmed until 19th century.

title: Death of Taqi al-Din al-Shami — Ottoman astronomer
description: Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ma'ruf, who built Istanbul's great observatory, died after Sultan Murad III ordered its destruction. His astronomical observations rivaled those of Tycho Brahe in accuracy.
outcome: Destruction of the observatory marked a decline in Ottoman astronomical research.

title: Battle of Rakka — Ottomans suppress Kurdish rebellion
description: Ottoman forces defeated a major Kurdish tribal confederation near Rakka that had rebelled against imperial authority. The victory consolidated Ottoman control over upper Mesopotamia.
outcome: Ottoman administration of Kurdish regions strengthened for the next century.

title: Battle of Wadi al-Makhazin — Moroccan victory preserves independence
description: Moroccan forces under Ahmad al-Mansur defeated a Portuguese invasion at Alcácer Quibir, killing King Sebastian I. This victory preserved Moroccan independence and enriched the Saadian dynasty with ransoms.
outcome: Morocco remained independent and emerged as a major power, eventually conquering Songhai.

title: Death of historian Mustafa Ali in Jeddah
description: Mustafa Ali of Gallipoli, Ottoman polymath and historian, died in Jeddah. His works including Künhü'l-Ahbar chronicle Ottoman history and offer critical insights into administrative corruption.
outcome: His critical historiography influenced Ottoman intellectual discourse.

title: Death of Sokollu Mehmed Pasha — end of Ottoman golden age
description: Sokollu Mehmed Pasha, Grand Vizier for fifteen years under three sultans, was assassinated in Istanbul. His tenure represented the peak of Ottoman administrative efficiency and military success.
outcome: His death marked the beginning of administrative decline in Ottoman governance.

title: Uzbeks under Abdullah Khan II conquer Herat
description: Uzbek Khan Abdullah II captured Herat from Safavid control, temporarily extending Bukhara's dominance over Khorasan. This represented the peak of Shaybanid power in Central Asia.
outcome: Safavids soon recaptured Herat, but Uzbek-Persian rivalry continued to shape Central Asian politics.

title: Completion of Ganj Ali Khan Complex in Kerman
description: Governor Ganj Ali Khan began construction of a grand public complex in Kerman including mosque, bathhouse, caravanserai, and bazaar. The complex exemplified Safavid urban planning and architectural synthesis.
outcome: The complex became a model for Safavid provincial architecture and remains a landmark of Persian urbanism.

title: Battle of Tondibi — Moroccan conquest of Songhai
description: Moroccan forces under Judar Pasha defeated Songhai Emperor Askia Ishaq II using firearms. This battle destroyed West Africa's last great Islamic empire and initiated Moroccan occupation of the Niger bend.
outcome: Songhai Empire collapsed, fragmenting West African Islamic power and disrupting trans-Saharan trade.

title: Bahmani Sultanate finally dissolves into five successor states
description: The Bahmani Sultanate of the Deccan formally ceased to exist as its provincial governors established independent kingdoms. The five Deccan sultanates—Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar—emerged as distinct powers.
outcome: Deccan sultanates remained independent Muslim powers until Mughal and Maratha conquests.

title: Abd al-Malik al-Asmai completes Manar al-Sabiqin in Yemen
description: Yemeni scholar Abd al-Malik al-Asmai completed his influential Sufi manual Manar al-Sabiqin, synthesizing Shafi'i jurisprudence with Sufi practice. The work became central to Yemeni Islamic education.
outcome: The text shaped Yemeni Sufism and jurisprudence for centuries.

title: Fall of Granada's last Muslim quarter
description: Following the 1492 conquest, forced conversions intensified under Cardinal Cisneros in Granada. Thousands of Muslims fled to North Africa while others became Moriscos under severe restrictions.
outcome: Mass migration created substantial Andalusian communities in Fez, Tunis, and Algiers.

title: Portuguese capture Muscat
description: Afonso de Albuquerque led Portuguese forces to capture the strategic port of Muscat in Oman. The conquest extended Portuguese control over Indian Ocean trade routes.
outcome: Portuguese dominance of the western Indian Ocean lasted until the 17th century.

title: Death of Shah Qasim al-Anbar in Najaf
description: The influential Sufi master Shah Qasim al-Anbar died in Najaf, having established the Qasimiyya order. His teachings influenced Safavid spirituality and Iraqi Shi'i piety.
sources: Kathryn Babayan, Mystics, Monarchs and Messiahs
outcome: The Qasimiyya order became prominent in Safavid-era Iraq.

title: Death of Mir Ali Shir Nava'i
description: The great Chagatai Turkish poet and statesman Mir Ali Shir Nava'i died in Herat. His literary works elevated Chagatai Turkish as a literary language alongside Persian.
sources: Maria E. Subtelny, Ali Shir Nava'i biography
outcome: Nava'i became the father of Turkic literature and Central Asian cultural renaissance.

title: Demak Sultanate defeats Majapahit remnants
description: Sultan Trenggana of Demak led forces that conquered the last Hindu-Buddhist strongholds in eastern Java. This completed the Islamization of Java's political landscape.
sources: M.C. Ricklefs, History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Java became predominantly Muslim with Islamic sultanates replacing Hindu kingdoms.

title: Death of Ismail I founder of Safavid dynasty
description: Shah Ismail I, who established the Safavid Empire and imposed Twelver Shi'ism as state religion, died in Ardabil. His son Tahmasp I succeeded him during a period of Ottoman pressure.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran under the Safavids
outcome: The Safavid transformation of Iran into a Shi'i state proved irreversible.

title: Death of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim al-Maghili
description: The influential North African scholar al-Maghili died after advising Songhay rulers on Islamic governance. His writings on political Islam and anti-Jewish polemics shaped West African Islam.
outcome: Al-Maghili's influence established rigorous Maliki jurisprudence in West Africa.

title: Ottoman conquest of Esztergom
description: Sultan Suleiman I captured the Hungarian fortress city of Esztergom, the ecclesiastical capital. This conquest deepened Ottoman control over central Hungary.
outcome: Hungary remained divided between Ottoman, Habsburg, and Transylvanian control until 1699.

title: Death of Sher Shah Suri
description: The Afghan ruler Sher Shah Suri died from injuries sustained during the siege of Kalinjar. His brief reign transformed North Indian administration and infrastructure through reforms.
sources: Abbas Khan Sarwani, Tarikh-i Sher Shahi
outcome: His administrative innovations were later adopted by the Mughal Empire.

title: Ahmad Gran receives Ottoman firearms
description: The Adal Sultanate under Ahmad Gran received Ottoman muskets and cannons via Yemen. Ottoman support enabled continued jihad against Christian Ethiopia.
outcome: Ottoman military technology temporarily shifted the balance in the Horn of Africa.

title: Death of scholar Ibn Iyas in Cairo
description: The Egyptian chronicler and historian Ibn Iyas died in Cairo after completing his masterwork Bada'i al-Zuhur. His history documented Mamluk Egypt's final decades and Ottoman conquest.
sources: Ibn Iyas, Bada'i al-Zuhur fi Waqa'i al-Duhur
outcome: Ibn Iyas preserved invaluable firsthand accounts of Mamluk society and conquest.

title: Death of Shaykh Ahmad Zarruq's successors
description: The Shadhili-Zarruqi Sufi order lost its leading figures in Misrata, Libya. The order had spread Shadhili teachings across North Africa under Ahmad Zarruq's reformed approach.
sources: Knut Vikør, Sufi and Scholar on the Desert Edge
outcome: Zarruqi principles shaped North African Sufism toward legal orthodoxy.

title: Death of Shaykh Bayazid Bistami II
description: The influential Naqshbandi Sufi master Shaykh Muhammad Tahir Bistami died in Chittagong. He established the Naqshbandi order's presence in Bengal.
sources: Richard Eaton, Rise of Islam on the Bengal Frontier
outcome: Naqshbandi networks facilitated Islam's spread in eastern Bengal.

title: Death of Sulayman Pasha al-Khadim
description: The powerful Ottoman Grand Vizier and administrator of Egypt, Sulayman Pasha, died in Cairo. He had reorganized Egyptian provincial administration and strengthened Ottoman control.
sources: Jane Hathaway, The Politics of Households in Ottoman Egypt
outcome: Ottoman provincial administration in Egypt stabilized after Mamluk traditions.

title: Portuguese establish Fort Jesus in Mombasa
description: Portuguese authorities built Fort Jesus in Mombasa to control East African coastal trade. The fortress design by Italian architect Cairati dominated the Swahili coast.
sources: James Kirkman, Fort Jesus: A Portuguese Fortress
outcome: The fort symbolized Portuguese interference in Swahili Muslim trade networks.

title: Alpujarras Rebellion of Moriscos begins
description: Moriscos in Granada rose in armed rebellion against forced Christian conversion and cultural suppression. The revolt lasted until 1571 under leaders like Aben Humeya.
outcome: The brutal suppression led to mass deportation of Moriscos from Granada.

title: Death of Selim II
description: Ottoman Sultan Selim II died in Constantinople after a reign marked by the Cyprus conquest and Lepanto defeat. His son Murad III succeeded amid financial challenges.
outcome: The transition began a period of increased court intrigue and harem politics.

title: Death of Tahmasp I of Persia
description: Safavid Shah Tahmasp I died after a 52-year reign that consolidated Shi'i identity in Iran. Civil war among his sons followed until Ismail II seized power.
outcome: Succession crises weakened Safavid power until Shah Abbas I's accession.

title: Ottomans begin Long Turkish War preparations
description: Ottoman Sultan Murad III initiated diplomatic and military preparations for conflict with Habsburg Austria. Border tensions in Hungary escalated toward full war.
sources: Gabor Ágoston, Guns for the Sultan
outcome: The Long War (1593-1606) drained Ottoman resources without territorial gains.

title: Death of Akbar's teacher Abd al-Latif Sultanpuri
description: The influential scholar and teacher Abd al-Latif Sultanpuri died at Akbar's court. He had taught the emperor Persian literature and Islamic sciences.
outcome: His teachings influenced Akbar's religious curiosity and syncretism.

title: Death of Sinan Pasha in Constantinople
description: The Ottoman Grand Vizier Koca Sinan Pasha died after multiple terms in office. His administrations included campaigns against Persia and governance reforms.
outcome: His career exemplified the power and vulnerability of Ottoman grand viziers.

title: Death of Baki Ottoman poet laureate
description: Mahmud Abdülbaki, known as Baki, died in Constantinople as the greatest Ottoman classical poet. His Persian-influenced Turkish poetry defined the high classical style.
sources: Walter Andrews, Poetry's Voice, Society's Song
outcome: Baki set the standard for Ottoman court poetry for generations.

title: Aceh Sultanate attacks Portuguese Melaka
description: Sultan Alauddin Riayat Shah al-Mukammil of Aceh launched a major naval expedition against Portuguese-held Melaka. The siege failed but demonstrated Acehnese naval power.
sources: Anthony Reid, Southeast Asia in the Age of Commerce
outcome: Aceh emerged as the leading Muslim power opposing Portuguese expansion in Southeast Asia.

title: Death of Ottoman astronomer Taqi al-Din
description: The renowned astronomer and engineer Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ma'ruf died in Constantinople. He had directed the Constantinople Observatory and made significant astronomical observations.
sources: Sevim Tekeli, The Observational Instruments of Istanbul Observatory
outcome: Despite his innovations, the observatory was demolished shortly after his death.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad III of Morocco
description: Saadian Sultan Muhammad al-Mutawakkil died in Marrakesh after a reign of civil strife. His death intensified succession conflicts within the Saadian dynasty.
sources: Jamil Abun-Nasr, A History of the Maghrib
outcome: Saadian fragmentation paved the way for eventual Alawite dynasty emergence.

title: Death of Sidi Yahya al-Tadili in Timbuktu
description: Sidi Yahya al-Tadili, influential scholar and imam of the Sankore Mosque in Timbuktu, dies after decades of teaching Islamic jurisprudence and hadith. His students became prominent scholars across the Sahel, cementing Timbuktu's status as a major center of Islamic learning.
outcome: His death marked the end of an era for Sankore Mosque scholarship before the Moroccan invasion.

title: Ottomans establish first Indian Ocean fleet at Suez
description: Sultan Suleiman orders the construction of a permanent naval base and arsenal at Suez under Admiral Selman Reis to project Ottoman power into the Indian Ocean. The fleet was built to counter Portuguese dominance of trade routes and support Muslim allies from Gujarat to Aceh.
sources: Katip Çelebi, Tuhfet ul-Kibar fi Esfar il-Bihar
outcome: Extended Ottoman naval reach to challenge Portuguese monopoly in the spice trade.

title: Death of Shaykh Ahmad Baba's teacher Muhammad Baghayogho
description: Muhammad Baghayogho al-Wangari, renowned Maliki jurist and teacher in Timbuktu, dies after training a generation of West African scholars. His most famous student, Ahmad Baba, would later become one of Africa's greatest Islamic scholars and write over 40 works.
outcome: His teaching lineage shaped Maliki scholarship in the Sahel for centuries.

title: Death of Shaykh Abd al-Wahhab al-Mutawakkil in Yemen
description: Abd al-Wahhab al-Mutawakkil, Zaydi scholar and Sufi master in Yemen, dies after establishing the Shadhili-influenced spiritual center in Zabid. He integrated Sufi practices with Zaydi theology and trained disciples across the Tihama coastal region.
outcome: His synthesis of Zaydism and Sufism influenced Yemeni spirituality for generations.

title: Completion of Badshahi Hammam in Lahore
description: The royal bathhouse (hammam) complex is completed in Lahore under Mughal Emperor Akbar's patronage, featuring Persian-influenced architectural design with elaborate frescoes. Built near the Lahore Fort, it served as both a public amenity and symbol of Mughal urban planning.
outcome: Demonstrated Mughal commitment to public infrastructure and Persian architectural traditions in South Asia.

title: Death of Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi in Tlemcen
description: Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi, influential Maghrebi theologian and author of major works on logic and Islamic jurisprudence, dies in Tlemcen. His commentaries on Ash'ari theology and his work on legal methodology remained standard texts in North African madrasas for centuries.
outcome: His theological works shaped Maliki-Ash'ari orthodoxy across the Maghreb and West Africa.

title: Sultan Ahmed I ascends Ottoman throne at age 13
description: Ahmed I became Ottoman Sultan at age 13 following the death of Mehmed III, breaking precedent by not executing his brother Mustafa. This decision ended the practice of fratricide in the Ottoman succession and established a new system of seniority.
outcome: Established the principle of preserving royal princes in the Kafes (cage) system rather than execution.

title: Final expulsion of Moriscos from Spain
description: King Philip III of Spain ordered the expulsion of all Moriscos (converted Muslims) from Spanish territories, affecting approximately 300,000 people. Most refugees fled to Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria, and the Ottoman Empire, bringing significant cultural and economic impact.
sources: Henri Lapeyre, Géographie de l'Espagne morisque
outcome: Ended nearly a millennium of Muslim presence in Iberia and caused severe demographic and economic consequences for Spain.

title: Ahmad al-Mansur's death fragments Saadian Morocco
description: The death of Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur led to civil war among his sons for control of the Saadian dynasty. The resulting instability weakened Morocco and eventually enabled the rise of the Alawite dynasty.
sources: Henri de Castries, Les sources inédites de l'histoire du Maroc
outcome: Initiated a period of fragmentation that lasted until the Alawite dynasty consolidated power in the 1660s.

title: Construction begins on Sultan Ahmed Mosque
description: Sultan Ahmed I commissioned the Blue Mosque in Constantinople, designed by architect Sedefkar Mehmed Agha. The mosque featured six minarets, which initially caused controversy as only the Masjid al-Haram in Mecca had that many.
sources: Gulru Necipoglu, The Age of Sinan
outcome: Became one of the most iconic Ottoman architectural achievements and a defining feature of Istanbul's skyline.

title: Shah Abbas I captures Kandahar from Mughals
description: Safavid Shah Abbas I successfully besieged and captured the strategic fortress city of Kandahar from Mughal Emperor Jahangir. The loss represented a significant territorial setback for the Mughals in Afghanistan.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Shifted the Safavid-Mughal border and remained a point of contention for decades.

title: Ottoman Sultan Osman II assassinated by Janissaries
description: Sultan Osman II was deposed and murdered by rebellious Janissaries after attempting to reform and reduce the power of the elite military corps. His death marked the first regicide in Ottoman history and demonstrated the growing power of military factions.
outcome: Initiated a period of instability and demonstrated the military's ability to overthrow sultans.

title: Shah Abbas I recaptures Baghdad from Ottomans
description: Safavid forces under Shah Abbas I successfully besieged Baghdad after a 39-day siege, taking the city from Ottoman control. The conquest was marked by significant violence against the Sunni population.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Temporarily shifted control of Mesopotamia to Safavid Persia until Ottoman reconquest in 1638.

title: Taj al-Din al-Subki completes major Hadith commentary
description: The Ottoman scholar and Hanafi jurist Taj al-Din al-Subki completed important works on Hadith methodology in Damascus. His scholarship contributed to the Ottoman tradition of Islamic jurisprudence during the classical period.
sources: Carl Brockelmann, Geschichte der arabischen Litteratur
outcome: Advanced Hadith studies in the Ottoman scholarly tradition.

title: Sultanate of Aceh reaches peak under Iskandar Muda
description: Sultan Iskandar Muda expanded the Aceh Sultanate to control most of Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula, making it the dominant maritime power in Southeast Asia. His reign saw flourishing trade, Islamic scholarship, and diplomatic relations with the Ottoman Empire.
sources: Denys Lombard, Le Sultanat d'Atjeh au temps d'Iskandar Muda
outcome: Established Aceh as the preeminent Islamic power in Southeast Asia and a rival to European colonial expansion.

title: Battle of Ahmedabad - Mughal forces defeat Gujarat rebellion
description: Mughal forces under Emperor Shah Jahan suppressed a major rebellion in Gujarat led by local nobles. The victory consolidated Mughal control over western India's prosperous trade routes.
outcome: Secured Mughal authority over Gujarat and its vital ports for decades.

title: Sultan Murad IV recaptures Baghdad for Ottomans
description: Ottoman Sultan Murad IV personally led the successful siege of Baghdad, retaking the city from Safavid control after 15 years. The sultan's military success restored Ottoman prestige and effectively ended major territorial conflicts with Persia.
outcome: Established the Ottoman-Safavid border that would last until World War I.

title: Dutch VOC captures Malacca from Portuguese
description: The Dutch East India Company captured Malacca after a five-month siege, ending 130 years of Portuguese control over the strategic Strait. The conquest affected Muslim trade networks and the Johor Sultanate's regional influence.
sources: M.C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Shifted European colonial power in Southeast Asia and altered Muslim maritime trade routes.

title: Mullah Sadra completes al-Hikma al-Mutaaliya
description: The Persian philosopher Mullah Sadra completed his masterwork 'Transcendent Theosophy' in Shiraz, synthesizing Islamic philosophy, Sufism, and theology. His work established a new school of Islamic philosophy combining Ibn Arabi's mysticism with Aristotelian logic.
sources: Seyyed Hossein Nasr, Sadr al-Din Shirazi and his Transcendent Theosophy
outcome: Became the foundation of the School of Isfahan and profoundly influenced Shi'a intellectual tradition.

title: Karposh Rebellion against Ottomans in Macedonia
description: A major Christian uprising led by Karposh erupted in Macedonia against Ottoman rule, supported by some Muslim Albanians. The rebellion was crushed, but it demonstrated growing tensions in Ottoman Balkans.
sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age
outcome: Revealed increasing administrative challenges in maintaining Ottoman control over Balkan territories.

title: War of Succession between sons of Shah Jahan
description: The Mughal War of Succession saw brothers Aurangzeb, Dara Shikoh, Shah Shuja, and Murad Baksh fight for the throne after Shah Jahan fell ill. Aurangzeb emerged victorious after the Battle of Samugarh and executed his brothers.
outcome: Established Aurangzeb's long reign but began the ideological shift away from Akbar's syncretic policies.

title: Moulay al-Rashid founds Alawite dynasty in Morocco
description: Moulay al-Rashid established the Alawite dynasty by conquering Fez and unifying Morocco after decades of fragmentation following Saadian collapse. He claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through his grandson Hassan.
sources: Jamil Abun-Nasr, A History of the Maghrib
outcome: Established the dynasty that continues to rule Morocco to the present day.

title: Great Fire of London impacts Ottoman-English trade
description: The Great Fire of London destroyed major warehouses holding Ottoman and Levantine goods, disrupting the established trade in silk, carpets, and coffee. Ottoman merchants in London's Turkish quarter suffered significant losses.
sources: Suraiya Faroqhi, The Ottoman Empire and the World Around It
outcome: Temporarily disrupted but did not permanently damage Ottoman-English commercial relations.

title: Ottomans complete conquest of Crete from Venice
description: After a 24-year siege, Ottoman forces under Grand Vizier Köprülü Fazıl Ahmed captured Candia (Heraklion), completing the conquest of Crete. The siege was one of the longest in history.
sources: Katib Celebi, Tuhfet ul-Kibar fi Esfar il-Bihar
outcome: Gave Ottomans control of a strategic Mediterranean island and demonstrated Ottoman naval power.

title: Sultan Agung's Great Mosque of Demak restored
description: Major restoration of the Great Mosque of Demak in Java, one of the oldest mosques in Indonesia, was completed under Mataram Sultanate patronage. The mosque represented the synthesis of Javanese and Islamic architectural traditions.
sources: M.C. Ricklefs, Mystic Synthesis in Java
outcome: Preserved an important symbol of Islamic heritage in Southeast Asia and Javanese Muslim identity.

title: Shabbetai Tzvi converts to Islam in Istanbul
description: The Jewish messianic claimant Shabbetai Tzvi converted to Islam under pressure from Sultan Mehmed IV, taking the name Aziz Mehmed Efendi. His conversion shocked his followers but created the unique Dönmeh community in the Ottoman Empire.
sources: Gershom Scholem, Sabbatai Sevi: The Mystical Messiah
outcome: Created the Dönmeh community that maintained a distinct identity within Ottoman society for centuries.

title: Aurangzeb reimplements jizya tax on non-Muslims
description: Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb controversially reinstated the jizya tax on non-Muslims after it had been abolished by Akbar over a century earlier. The policy caused widespread protests and damaged Hindu-Muslim relations.
outcome: Marked a significant departure from Mughal syncretism and contributed to increased Hindu resistance.

title: Moroccan Sultan Moulay Ismail begins 55-year reign
description: Moulay Ismail became Sultan of Morocco and began consolidating Alawite power through military campaigns and administrative reforms. He created a professional slave army and established diplomatic relations with European powers.
sources: Dominique Busnot, Histoire du règne de Moulay Ismail
outcome: Unified Morocco and established it as a significant regional power resisting Ottoman and European encroachment.

title: Ottomans lose Buda after 145 years of rule
description: Habsburg forces under Charles of Lorraine captured Buda from the Ottomans during the Great Turkish War. The loss of Hungary's historic capital marked a major territorial setback for Ottoman power in Europe.
sources: Rifa'at Abou-El-Haj, The 1703 Rebellion and the Structure of Ottoman Politics
outcome: Began the permanent Ottoman retreat from Hungary and shift in European balance of power.

title: Battle of Zernest - Ottomans defeat Transylvanian-Habsburg forces
description: Ottoman forces under Grand Vizier Köprülüzade Fazıl Mustafa Pasha defeated a combined Transylvanian-Habsburg army at Zernest. The victory temporarily halted Habsburg advances in the Balkans during the Great Turkish War.
sources: Joseph von Hammer-Purgstall, Geschichte des Osmanischen Reiches
outcome: Provided temporary respite but could not reverse Ottoman territorial losses in the Balkans.

title: Treaty of Karlowitz - Ottoman territorial losses formalized
description: The Treaty of Karlowitz ended the Great Turkish War with the Ottomans ceding Hungary, Transylvania, and significant Balkan territories to the Habsburgs and Poland. It was the first major treaty where the Ottomans negotiated as the losing party.
sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age
outcome: Marked the beginning of Ottoman territorial decline in Europe and shift from expansion to defensive posture.

title: Safavid Shah Abbas I moves capital to Isfahan
description: Shah Abbas I permanently relocates the Safavid capital from Qazvin to Isfahan, initiating massive urban development. The move transforms Isfahan into one of the world's most magnificent cities with new boulevards, palaces, and mosques.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Isfahan became a cultural and commercial center rivaling Constantinople and Paris in splendor.

title: Death of Mughal Emperor Akbar the Great
description: Emperor Akbar dies in Agra after ruling for nearly 50 years, having expanded the Mughal Empire across most of the Indian subcontinent. His son Jahangir succeeds him, inheriting a wealthy and administratively sophisticated empire.
outcome: Akbar's death marked the transition from expansion to consolidation in Mughal history.

title: Sultan Iskandar Muda ascends throne of Aceh Sultanate
description: Iskandar Muda becomes Sultan of Aceh in northern Sumatra, beginning a reign that would establish Aceh as the dominant maritime power in Southeast Asia. Under his rule, Aceh controlled both sides of the Strait of Malacca and challenged Portuguese dominance.
outcome: Aceh reached its territorial and commercial zenith, becoming the most powerful Islamic state in Southeast Asia.

title: Battle of Swally — Mughals allow English trading rights
description: English East India Company ships defeat a Portuguese fleet off Surat in the Battle of Swally near the Gujarat coast. Mughal Emperor Jahangir subsequently grants the English trading privileges, beginning significant British commercial presence in India.
sources: William Foster, The English Factories in India
outcome: The English victory opened Mughal India to British trade, laying groundwork for eventual colonization.

title: Naqshbandi Sheikh Ahmad Sirhindi imprisoned by Jahangir
description: Mughal Emperor Jahangir imprisons the influential Naqshbandi Sufi leader Ahmad Sirhindi in Gwalior Fort for refusing to prostrate before the emperor. Sirhindi had criticized Akbar's religious syncretism and advocated a return to orthodox Islamic practice.
outcome: Sirhindi's imprisonment and later release increased his influence, shaping orthodox Islamic reform movements in India.

title: Construction of Shah Abbas I's Masjid-i Shah begins
description: Shah Abbas I commissions the construction of Masjid-i Shah (later Imam Mosque) on the southern side of Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahan Square. The mosque would become one of the masterpieces of Persian architecture with its stunning tilework and innovative acoustics.
outcome: The mosque epitomized Safavid architectural achievement and remains one of Iran's most iconic structures.

title: Banten Sultanate attacks Dutch VOC factory
description: Forces of the Banten Sultanate in Java attack Dutch VOC trading posts, resisting European commercial encroachment in the spice trade. Sultan Abdul Mufakhir led efforts to maintain Banten's control over regional commerce against Dutch expansion.
sources: H.J. de Graaf, De regering van Sultan Agung
outcome: Despite resistance, Dutch commercial and military power gradually eroded Banten's independence over the century.

title: Ottoman recapture of Baghdad from Safavids
description: Ottoman forces under Sultan Osman II recapture Baghdad from the Safavids after Shah Abbas I had taken it in 1623. The city would change hands several times in the Ottoman-Safavid rivalry over Mesopotamia.
outcome: Baghdad remained a contested prize between the Ottoman and Safavid empires throughout the century.

title: Death of Sultan Ahmed I — Mustafa I becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Ottoman Sultan Ahmed I dies at age 27, and his mentally unstable brother Mustafa I is placed on the throne by the Janissaries. This began a period of political instability with rapid succession of weak sultans and powerful regents.
outcome: The succession crisis initiated the 'Sultanate of Women' period where valide sultans wielded significant power.

title: Death of Shah Abbas I the Great
description: Shah Abbas I, the greatest Safavid ruler, dies after transforming Persia into a major military and commercial power. His successors would prove unable to maintain his administrative and military reforms, beginning gradual Safavid decline.
sources: Iskandar Beg Munshi, Tarikh-e Alam-Ara-ye Abbasi
outcome: Abbas I's death marked the beginning of Safavid decline as weaker rulers failed to sustain his achievements.

title: Mumtaz Mahal dies — Shah Jahan orders Taj Mahal
description: Mughal Empress Mumtaz Mahal dies in childbirth in Burhanpur, devastating Emperor Shah Jahan. He immediately orders construction of the Taj Mahal in Agra as her mausoleum, which would become the world's most famous Islamic monument.
outcome: The Taj Mahal became an enduring symbol of Mughal architectural achievement and romantic devotion.

title: Sultan Murad IV captures Baghdad from Safavids
description: Ottoman Sultan Murad IV personally leads the army that recaptures Baghdad after a 40-day siege, ending Persian control that had lasted since 1623. Murad's victory restored Ottoman prestige and secured Mesopotamia for the empire.
outcome: Baghdad remained under Ottoman control until World War I, with the 1639 Treaty of Zuhab formalizing borders.

title: Death of Safavid philosopher Mir Damad
description: Muhammad Baqir ibn Muhammad, known as Mir Damad, dies in Najaf after establishing the Isfahan School of Islamic philosophy. As teacher of Mulla Sadra, he synthesized Peripatetic philosophy, Illuminationist thought, and theology.
outcome: Mir Damad's work formed the foundation of the Isfahan School, influencing Shi'i thought for centuries.

title: Mataram Sultanate reaches greatest extent under Amangkurat I
description: Amangkurat I becomes Sultan of Mataram in central Java, ruling the most powerful Islamic state on the island. His reign saw Mataram's maximum territorial control but also increasing internal tensions and Dutch interference.
outcome: Despite territorial expansion, Amangkurat's harsh rule sowed seeds of rebellion that weakened the sultanate.

title: Death of Sufi poet and scholar Abd al-Ghani al-Nabulsi born
description: Abd al-Ghani al-Nabulsi is born in Damascus, who would become one of the most prolific Sufi writers of the Ottoman period. He authored over 200 works on mysticism, theology, and travel, defending Ibn Arabi's doctrines.
outcome: Nabulsi became the foremost defender of Akbarian Sufism in the Ottoman world, traveling extensively and writing prolifically.

title: Köprülü Mehmed Pasha becomes Ottoman Grand Vizier
description: Köprülü Mehmed Pasha becomes Grand Vizier at age 71, beginning the Köprülü era of Ottoman revival. Despite his advanced age, he ruthlessly restored central authority, executing thousands of corrupt officials and rebels.
outcome: The Köprülü family dominated Ottoman politics for decades, temporarily reversing imperial decline.

title: Aurangzeb defeats Dara Shikoh at Battle of Samugarh
